questions.question: SaulėGrid ran download, validate, and aggregate as separate cron jobs at 00:00, 00:30, and 01:00. What is the fundamental limitation of this design?
options: The schedule encodes a timing assumption rather than a real dependency between the steps
Cron cannot run Python scripts and that forces SaulėGrid to rewrite jobs as bash one-liners
Each cron entry is locked to UTC and that breaks every pipeline during DST transitions
Cron runs jobs only once per host and that prevents SaulėGrid from scaling horizontally
questions.correctAnswer: The schedule encodes a timing assumption rather than a real dependency between the steps
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: cron answers 'when' (run at 00:30) — not 'is it safe to run' (did download finish). The 30-min buffer was an assumption that breaks when reality changes.

questions.question: Without an orchestrator, when one of SaulėGrid's twelve pipeline steps fails at 03:00, what does the on-call engineer have to do?
options: Mentally trace the dependency chain and manually rerun every downstream step in order
Restart the entire host machine to clear corrupted state from the failed job process
Open a support ticket with the cloud vendor since cron logs are owned by the platform
Wait for the next scheduled tick and rely on cron to retry the failed job by itself
questions.correctAnswer: Mentally trace the dependency chain and manually rerun every downstream step in order
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: without an orchestrator there is no automatic dependency resolution. The operator has to figure out the failure point and rerun downstream steps by hand.

questions.question: Lesson 3 names three pillars of orchestration that must work together. Which trio is correct?
options: Dependency management, state tracking, and automatic failure recovery across runs
Source control, secret management, and continuous deployment of the pipeline code
Data validation, schema evolution, and quality reporting on every produced dataset
Resource quotas, autoscaling rules, and cost dashboards for every pipeline step
questions.correctAnswer: Dependency management, state tracking, and automatic failure recovery across runs
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: dependency management decides order, state tracking detects failures, recovery fixes them. Each is incomplete without the others.

questions.question: Why did Airflow become the industry standard despite Luigi and Oozie being older?
options: Python-native DAGs, a rich web UI, and a community that built provider integrations
It was the only orchestrator licensed for free under a permissive open-source license
Microsoft and Google jointly funded its development and bundled it with their clouds
Its DAG syntax was simpler than every alternative and required no configuration files
questions.correctAnswer: Python-native DAGs, a rich web UI, and a community that built provider integrations
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: Airflow combined Python-native definition + web UI + a strong open-source ecosystem at the right time. None of those alone were unique — the combination was.

questions.question: Lesson 5 compares Airflow to a conductor. What does that analogy mean in practice?
options: Airflow coordinates other tools at the right time but does no data processing itself
Airflow replaces PySpark and dbt by absorbing their transformation logic into DAGs
Airflow rewrites every downstream tool's queries to optimize end-to-end performance
Airflow holds the data in memory and streams it through each tool in the data stack
questions.correctAnswer: Airflow coordinates other tools at the right time but does no data processing itself
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: Airflow is the conductor — it tells PySpark when to run and SFTP when to download but does no processing or transfer itself.

questions.question: Why does the SaulėGrid course use the same company, columns, and pipeline across every lesson?
options: Familiarity frees mental energy to focus on the new Airflow concept each lesson teaches
Lithuanian regulations require all training material to use a single named example company
It avoids per-example licensing fees that come from using multiple fictional brand names
Airflow only supports DAGs that share a topic and breaks if datasets differ between DAGs
questions.correctAnswer: Familiarity frees mental energy to focus on the new Airflow concept each lesson teaches
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: by Module 5 the learner knows meter_id and daily_kw without reading column descriptions, so the new concept gets the attention.

questions.question: A new Airflow user wants to read a CSV, clean rows, and compute aggregates inside a single PythonOperator task. Why is this an anti-pattern at production scale?
options: Airflow tasks should trigger external engines like PySpark — not perform the heavy work
PythonOperator does not have access to the filesystem and cannot read CSV files at all
Airflow taxes per-row processing inside tasks and the bill grows linearly with row count
DAG files cannot import pandas because the scheduler blocks any non-Airflow imports
questions.correctAnswer: Airflow tasks should trigger external engines like PySpark — not perform the heavy work
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: DAG code is lightweight orchestration logic. Heavy transformation belongs in PySpark/SQL/dbt that the task triggers, not in the task body itself.

questions.question: What do the three letters in 'DAG' mean for how Airflow can run your pipeline?
options: Directed gives order, Acyclic prevents loops, Graph enables automatic parallel execution
Distributed shards work, Atomic isolates writes, Generated schedules cron expressions
Daemon runs forever, Async handles I/O, Generic accepts any Python callable as a task
Deferred runs later, Async sends signals, Granular splits work into many small slices
questions.correctAnswer: Directed gives order, Acyclic prevents loops, Graph enables automatic parallel execution
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: directed = order, acyclic = no infinite loops, graph = the structure that lets the scheduler find parallel opportunities automatically.

questions.question: A junior engineer wants to push a 200MB DataFrame from one task to the next using XCom. What should they do instead?
options: Write the DataFrame to storage in one task and pass only the file path through XCom
Compress the DataFrame with gzip before pushing it through XCom to reduce payload size
Split the DataFrame into 1MB chunks and push each chunk as a separate XCom record
Increase the metadata database size and the XCom row limit to accept larger payloads
questions.correctAnswer: Write the DataFrame to storage in one task and pass only the file path through XCom
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: XCom is for small values (paths, counts, flags). Large data goes to storage; XCom carries the path. Anything else hammers the metadata DB.

questions.question: Lesson 10 outlines what Module 2 will teach. Which area does it cover?
options: How Airflow's four components work together to discover, schedule, and execute DAGs
How to write production-grade PySpark transformation logic for the Silver layer
How to design a data warehouse schema that supports SaulėGrid's billing reports
How to build a custom executor that replaces the default Celery executor at scale
questions.correctAnswer: How Airflow's four components work together to discover, schedule, and execute DAGs
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: the next module makes the orchestration concepts concrete by walking through the scheduler, executor, database, and web server.

questions.question: How does Module 2's mnemonic describe the four core Airflow components?
options: The scheduler decides, the executor does, the database remembers, and the web server shows
The scheduler queues, the executor logs, the database backs up, and the web server proxies
The scheduler validates, the executor authenticates, the database caches, and the UI alerts
The scheduler compiles, the executor optimizes, the database indexes, and the UI templates
questions.correctAnswer: The scheduler decides, the executor does, the database remembers, and the web server shows
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: this mnemonic maps each component to its single responsibility. When debugging, decide which of the four 'verbs' the problem belongs to.

questions.question: An engineer puts `df = pd.read_csv('huge.csv')` at the top of a DAG file. What happens at SaulėGrid scale?
options: The CSV is read on every scheduler parse cycle, wasting CPU and slowing every DAG
The scheduler caches the DataFrame after one read and reuses it for every DAG Run
The scheduler refuses to parse the file and surfaces a clear error in the web UI
The CSV is loaded once at startup and stays in memory until the host is restarted
questions.correctAnswer: The CSV is read on every scheduler parse cycle, wasting CPU and slowing every DAG
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: the scheduler executes the DAG file as a Python module on every loop. Top-level heavy code runs over and over — keep DAG files lightweight.

questions.question: If the Airflow web server crashes for an hour, what is the impact on running pipelines?
options: Pipelines keep running because the scheduler and executor do not depend on the web server
All pipelines pause until the web server is back online and reissues task heartbeat signals
All running tasks fail because they cannot read connection passwords from the web server
Pipelines downgrade to sequential execution because the web server distributes worker slots
questions.correctAnswer: Pipelines keep running because the scheduler and executor do not depend on the web server
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: the web server is for visibility. Losing it removes the UI but the scheduler keeps queueing and the executor keeps running tasks.

questions.question: What kind of data does the Airflow metadata database store?
options: Operational state — DAG runs, task instances, connections — not the pipeline's actual data
Both operational state and the raw output rows produced by every task in every DAG Run
Encrypted Parquet copies of the Bronze layer for fast lookup from the Airflow web server
Cached query results from PostgreSQL warehouses to accelerate dashboard rendering jobs
questions.correctAnswer: Operational state — DAG runs, task instances, connections — not the pipeline's actual data
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: the metadata DB holds Airflow's own bookkeeping. SaulėGrid's meter readings live in the data lake — putting them in the metadata DB degrades Airflow.

questions.question: SaulėGrid runs on a single beefy machine with parallel pipelines. Which executor is the right fit?
options: LocalExecutor with PostgreSQL — parallel tasks on one machine without distributed pieces
SequentialExecutor with SQLite — easiest to set up and matches the single-machine deploy
CeleryExecutor with Redis — distributes work across workers even on a single machine
KubernetesExecutor — every task spins up its own pod for maximum process isolation
questions.correctAnswer: LocalExecutor with PostgreSQL — parallel tasks on one machine without distributed pieces
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: LocalExecutor parallelizes tasks across processes on one machine. Sequential cannot parallelize; Celery/Kubernetes is overkill for a single host.

questions.question: An engineer adds a new DAG file with a syntax error at the top. What does Airflow do?
options: The DAG silently disappears from the UI until the syntax error in the file is fixed
The scheduler emails every admin in the metadata database with the full Python traceback
Airflow prints the syntax error to the home page banner and lists the file as 'broken'
The scheduler quarantines all DAGs in the same directory until the bad file is removed
questions.correctAnswer: The DAG silently disappears from the UI until the syntax error in the file is fixed
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: the DAG directory is the source of truth, but a syntax error makes the parser crash for that file — the DAG disappears with no in-UI explanation.

questions.question: In Airflow's component communication, which path is the only direct scheduler-to-executor link?
options: The task queue — the scheduler enqueues task instances and the executor picks them up
A WebSocket from the scheduler that the executor opens whenever a heartbeat is needed
A gRPC stream that the executor maintains to receive new tasks in real-time order
A symbolic link in the DAG directory the scheduler updates with new task descriptors
questions.correctAnswer: The task queue — the scheduler enqueues task instances and the executor picks them up
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: every other path goes through the metadata DB. Only the queue is a direct scheduler→executor channel.

questions.question: Why is `airflow standalone` recommended for local development but not production?
options: It uses SequentialExecutor, which runs tasks one at a time and cannot parallelize them
It refuses to load DAGs that contain more than ten tasks defined in the same DAG file
It limits all schedules to hourly or slower and rejects any sub-hour cron expression
It blocks every external connection and only allows tasks targeting the local filesystem
questions.correctAnswer: It uses SequentialExecutor, which runs tasks one at a time and cannot parallelize them
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: standalone mode is convenient (single command, SQLite) but Sequential prevents parallelism. Production needs LocalExecutor + PostgreSQL or beyond.

questions.question: In the lifecycle of a task instance, which sequence of states is the happy path?
options: no_status → scheduled → queued → running → success
no_status → running → scheduled → queued → success
queued → no_status → running → scheduled → success
scheduled → no_status → queued → success → running
questions.correctAnswer: no_status → scheduled → queued → running → success
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: scheduler creates (no_status), evaluates eligibility (scheduled), enqueues (queued), executor picks up (running), task finishes (success).

questions.question: Lesson 10 contrasts retries and human intervention. What is each one's job?
options: Retries handle transient failures automatically — permanent failures need a human fix
Retries handle every failure type and the on-call engineer is paged only for outages
Retries handle bugs in the code and humans handle network blips that retries cannot fix
Retries delete the failed run and humans rebuild it from scratch using the backfill CLI
questions.correctAnswer: Retries handle transient failures automatically — permanent failures need a human fix
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: a retry on a code bug fails the same way each time. Retries are for transient problems; permanent ones require code or config changes.

questions.question: What is the difference between a DAG and a DAG Run in Airflow's terminology?
options: A DAG is the code in your Python file; a DAG Run is one execution recorded in the DB
A DAG is one Python function and a DAG Run is the queue entry that triggers that function
A DAG is a finished pipeline and a DAG Run is the unfinished version still being authored
A DAG is a scheduled task and a DAG Run is the executor process that runs that task
questions.correctAnswer: A DAG is the code in your Python file; a DAG Run is one execution recorded in the DB
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: DAG = definition (your code). DAG Run = one instance of that DAG executing for a given logical date.

questions.question: What is wrong with `python_callable=download_from_sftp()` in a PythonOperator definition?
options: Parentheses call the function during DAG parsing — every loop instead of once per run
Parentheses make the function private and the executor cannot find it at task runtime
Parentheses cause Python to treat the result as a string and the operator rejects it
Parentheses lock the function reference so Airflow cannot retry the task on failure
questions.correctAnswer: Parentheses call the function during DAG parsing — every loop instead of once per run
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: pass the function reference (no parens). With parens, Python calls the function during parsing — every few seconds.

questions.question: Why does SaulėGrid prefer the `with DAG()` context-manager pattern over the explicit `dag=dag` pattern?
options: Tasks inside the with-block automatically belong to the DAG — forgetting becomes impossible
The context manager runs tasks faster because it avoids creating a separate DAG object
The context manager allows multiple DAG IDs in one block which simplifies deployment
The context manager bypasses the metadata database and writes directly to the worker disk
questions.correctAnswer: Tasks inside the with-block automatically belong to the DAG — forgetting becomes impossible
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: with-block makes ownership automatic; the dag=dag pattern requires it on every task and is easy to forget.

questions.question: Why is `start_date=datetime.now()` the most common DAG misconfiguration?
options: The start_date keeps moving forward on every parse so no DAG Run is ever scheduled
datetime.now() raises a TypeError because Airflow needs a date object, not a datetime
The scheduler caches the first value and ignores it forever — the DAG never updates
datetime.now() is naive UTC so the scheduler runs every DAG twice per day to compensate
questions.correctAnswer: The start_date keeps moving forward on every parse so no DAG Run is ever scheduled
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: start_date is evaluated on every parse. With now(), the baseline shifts every few seconds and the scheduler can never decide a run is due.

questions.question: What is the role of `default_args` on a DAG definition?
options: It encodes team operational standards (retries, timeouts, owner) inherited by every task
It overrides the schedule parameter and replaces it with task-specific cron expressions
It determines which executor the DAG uses regardless of the global Airflow configuration
It registers the DAG with the metadata database so the web server can render it in the UI
questions.correctAnswer: It encodes team operational standards (retries, timeouts, owner) inherited by every task
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: default_args = the default policy (owner, retries, retry_delay, email). Tasks inherit it; individual tasks override only when they need to.

questions.question: Two files in SaulėGrid's dags/ directory accidentally use the same dag_id. What happens?
options: The second DAG is silently ignored — no error, no log, just missing from the web UI
The scheduler refuses to start until the conflict between the two files is resolved
Airflow renames the second DAG by appending a numeric suffix to its dag_id automatically
Both DAGs run normally because the metadata database stores them as separate entries
questions.correctAnswer: The second DAG is silently ignored — no error, no log, just missing from the web UI
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: this silent behavior is one of Airflow's most frustrating debugging experiences. Always grep the codebase before assigning a dag_id.

questions.question: An engineer writes `import pandas as pd` at the top of every DAG file. Why does this hurt the scheduler?
options: The import runs on every parse cycle, consuming scheduler CPU for every DAG file
Pandas is incompatible with Airflow's Python and crashes the scheduler at startup time
The import locks the metadata database for several seconds during each scheduler parse
Pandas tries to phone home to its update server and the scheduler waits on the response
questions.correctAnswer: The import runs on every parse cycle, consuming scheduler CPU for every DAG file
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: top-level code runs every parse. Heavy imports (pandas, requests, boto3) belong inside task callables — not at module level.

questions.question: When investigating a failed pipeline, what is the standard Airflow debugging hierarchy?
options: DAG Run → Task Instance → Log
Connection → Variable → Pool
Worker → Pod → Container
Trigger → Sensor → Operator
questions.correctAnswer: DAG Run → Task Instance → Log
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: every UI view is organized around DAG Run → Task Instance → Log. This top-down workflow narrows the question on every step.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `doc_md` field on a DAG definition?
options: Document why decisions were made so the future on-call engineer can understand the DAG
Render the DAG's source code as syntax-highlighted Markdown on the Airflow home page
Replace the doc strings on each Python callable since Airflow ignores Python docstrings
Generate a JSON schema describing the DAG's inputs for downstream consumer dashboards
questions.correctAnswer: Document why decisions were made so the future on-call engineer can understand the DAG
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: doc_md captures the why (why retries=5? why this schedule?). Six months later, the answers belong here, not in tribal memory.

questions.question: Why does SaulėGrid use a flat `dags/` directory plus a `helpers/` subdirectory?
options: DAGs are independently deployable, while shared helper code lives in importable modules
Airflow forbids more than two levels of nesting in the DAG directory at the filesystem level
A flat layout lets the scheduler bypass module imports and load DAGs directly from disk
Subdirectories named anything other than 'helpers' are excluded from scheduler parsing
questions.correctAnswer: DAGs are independently deployable, while shared helper code lives in importable modules
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: flat dags/ keeps each DAG its own deploy unit; helpers/ holds shared utilities (sftp_utils, validation, alerting) that DAGs import.

questions.question: What does the 'single-responsibility' principle mean for a SaulėGrid task design?
options: A task does exactly one thing — download_meters downloads, it does not also validate
A task is owned by exactly one engineer who is paged on every failure of that task
A task uses exactly one Connection at runtime so credential rotation is straightforward
A task lives in exactly one DAG and is never reused across multiple pipeline files
questions.correctAnswer: A task does exactly one thing — download_meters downloads, it does not also validate
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: a task that downloads, validates, and aggregates is debugged, retried, and monitored as three things. One responsibility per task is easier to reason about.

questions.question: What is the relationship between an Operator and a Task in Airflow?
options: Operator is the class (template); Task is an instance produced by configuring the operator
Operator is the running process; Task is the Python file that defines the operator subclass
Operator is one task in production; Task is the schedule entry that triggers that operator
Operator is the database row; Task is the worker process that updates that row at runtime
questions.correctAnswer: Operator is the class (template); Task is an instance produced by configuring the operator
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: PythonOperator is a class. Task = an instantiation of that class with a specific task_id and parameters. Many tasks can share one operator class.

questions.question: BashOperator's `bash_command='/scripts/download.sh'` raises a 'template not found' error. What is the fix?
options: Add a trailing space — without it Airflow tries to render the path as a Jinja template
Change the path to use a forward slash on every OS instead of platform-specific separators
Set the bash_command to a Python f-string so Jinja templating is bypassed by Airflow
Wrap the bash_command in triple quotes so Airflow treats it as raw shell input only
questions.correctAnswer: Add a trailing space — without it Airflow tries to render the path as a Jinja template
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: when bash_command ends with a path, Airflow assumes it is a Jinja template path. A single trailing space tells Airflow it is a literal command.

questions.question: Inside a PythonOperator callable, the file is missing. What should the callable do?
options: Raise an exception so Airflow can fail the task and trigger retries plus alerts
Return None and write a warning to stdout so the next task can decide what to do
Print the error message and continue execution so downstream tasks can still run
Catch the error silently and write an empty placeholder file to keep the chain going
questions.correctAnswer: Raise an exception so Airflow can fail the task and trigger retries plus alerts
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: silent failures are the most expensive failures. Raise an exception — Airflow's retry and alerting handle the rest.

questions.question: Why does SaulėGrid add EmptyOperator checkpoints (start, bronze_ready, transforms_done, end)?
options: They cost nothing at runtime but make the Graph View structure visually clear
They reset the metadata database state between phases to avoid stale task entries
They throttle the executor so downstream tasks do not start before upstream is durable
They emit Slack notifications between phases without needing a separate notify task
questions.correctAnswer: They cost nothing at runtime but make the Graph View structure visually clear
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: an EmptyOperator runs in milliseconds and uses no real resources. The benefit — clearer phases in the Graph View — is significant.

questions.question: Network-dependent download, local validation, and Slack notification — which retry/timeout policy fits?
options: Download: many retries with backoff and a long timeout. Validate: short timeout and few retries. Notify: no retries and short timeout.
Download: no retries since SFTP is reliable. Validate: long timeout because validation is slow. Notify: many retries because Slack is flaky.
Download: short timeout to fail fast. Validate: many retries because data quality varies. Notify: long timeout because Slack waits for ack.
questions.correctAnswer: Download: many retries with backoff and a long timeout. Validate: short timeout and few retries. Notify: no retries and short timeout.
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: per-task config encodes the team's operational experience. SFTP is slow & flaky → long timeout + retries. Validation is local → short. Notify must not block.

questions.question: Why does SaulėGrid use the verb_noun naming convention for task IDs (download_meters, validate_readings)?
options: Names tell you what the task does — task_1, step_a, do_stuff are meaningless in the UI
Airflow rejects task IDs that do not start with a verb because of its naming linter
The metadata database indexes verb prefixes for faster filtering across DAGs in the UI
Verb_noun is required for TaskFlow tasks because the decorator parses the task ID format
questions.correctAnswer: Names tell you what the task does — task_1, step_a, do_stuff are meaningless in the UI
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: task_id appears in the Graph View, in logs, in alerts. A descriptive verb_noun name explains the task at a glance.

questions.question: Why does SaulėGrid set `execution_timeout` on every production task — even ones that 'should never hang'?
options: A task with no timeout that hangs runs forever; a 15-min timeout catches it in 15 minutes
The default timeout is 30 seconds and trips on every task longer than half a minute
Airflow refuses to schedule any task without an execution_timeout in production mode
Setting a timeout is required for retries to fire because retries inherit the timeout value
questions.correctAnswer: A task with no timeout that hangs runs forever; a 15-min timeout catches it in 15 minutes
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: 'should never hang' is not a guarantee. A timeout converts an unbounded outage into a bounded one with retry/alert behavior.

questions.question: SaulėGrid wants Slack to be notified whether the pipeline succeeded or failed. Which trigger rule fits the notify task?
options: trigger_rule='all_done' so notify runs after the pipeline regardless of success or failure
trigger_rule='all_success' so notify only runs when the pipeline finished perfectly clean
trigger_rule='one_failed' so notify only fires when at least one upstream task has failed
trigger_rule='dummy' so notify always runs even before any upstream tasks have completed
questions.correctAnswer: trigger_rule='all_done' so notify runs after the pipeline regardless of success or failure
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: all_done = upstream are all in a final state (success or failed). Perfect for a status notification that should fire either way.

questions.question: Module 4's complete Bronze DAG has check_sftp before download_meters. Why this 'heartbeat' pattern?
options: Fail fast in 2 minutes if SFTP is down instead of waiting 60 minutes for download to time out
Warm up the SFTP connection pool so the actual download has a sub-second establishment phase
Authenticate once with SFTP so the credentials can be cached for downstream tasks to reuse
Pre-fetch the SFTP file index so the download task does not have to issue a LIST command
questions.correctAnswer: Fail fast in 2 minutes if SFTP is down instead of waiting 60 minutes for download to time out
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: heartbeat = a cheap precondition check before an expensive operation. Fails fast on SFTP outages, saving wasted retry time.

questions.question: In the classic operator pattern, what is the boilerplate that TaskFlow eliminates?
options: Manual `kwargs['ti'].xcom_push(...)` and `xcom_pull(...)` calls inside every callable
Manual `dag=dag` arguments on every operator definition you write inside the file
Manual `task_id=...` arguments since TaskFlow auto-generates names from function bodies
Manual `default_args` dictionaries since TaskFlow injects them at parse time globally
questions.correctAnswer: Manual `kwargs['ti'].xcom_push(...)` and `xcom_pull(...)` calls inside every callable
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: classic ops require manual XCom push/pull. TaskFlow's @task makes return values and parameters do that automatically.

questions.question: What does the `@task` decorator turn a regular Python function into?
options: An Airflow task with the same capabilities as a PythonOperator — retries, timeouts, logs
A new operator class that subclasses BaseOperator and must be registered with the scheduler
A scheduler-side validator that runs the function during parsing instead of at runtime
A web-server endpoint that serves the function's return value over HTTP to other DAGs
questions.correctAnswer: An Airflow task with the same capabilities as a PythonOperator — retries, timeouts, logs
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: @task wraps the function as a task with all PythonOperator parameters. Same execution model, friendlier syntax.

questions.question: A junior engineer's @task function returns a 500MB pandas DataFrame. What is the correct fix?
options: Write the DataFrame to storage and return only the file path through the XCom return value
Compress the DataFrame using pickle protocol 5 before returning it to keep XCom under quota
Split the DataFrame into many smaller @task return values that XCom can handle separately
Increase the metadata database size so XCom can store payloads above the default limit
questions.correctAnswer: Write the DataFrame to storage and return only the file path through the XCom return value
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: TaskFlow returns are XCom under the hood. SaulėGrid's rule: small values only — paths, counts, flags. Large data goes to storage.

questions.question: In TaskFlow, writing `count = validate_meter_data(path)` does what to the dependency graph?
options: Both wires the dependency (validate runs after download) and binds the data (path flows in)
Wires the dependency only — the data binding requires a separate xcom_pull call afterward
Binds the data only — the >> operator must still be added explicitly to wire the dependency
Neither — the assignment must be wrapped with airflow.dag.connect() to take any effect
questions.correctAnswer: Both wires the dependency (validate runs after download) and binds the data (path flows in)
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: in TaskFlow, calling a @task function creates the task AND wires the dependency in one expression. Data flow IS the dependency graph.

questions.question: A `@task(multiple_outputs=True)` function returns a list. What happens?
options: Airflow raises an error at runtime — multiple_outputs requires the function to return a dict
Airflow uses each list element as a separate XCom under keys '0', '1', '2' automatically
Airflow wraps the list as the value of a single XCom under the function's task_id key
Airflow ignores multiple_outputs and stores the list as a single return value as usual
questions.correctAnswer: Airflow raises an error at runtime — multiple_outputs requires the function to return a dict
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: multiple_outputs=True requires a dict because the dict's keys become XCom keys. Lists, ints, strings raise at runtime.

questions.question: In a SaulėGrid DAG, three tasks pass file paths between Python callables. Which style fits best?
options: TaskFlow @task — data passes between Python tasks; visible in the function signatures
Classic PythonOperator — manual xcom_push/pull is more explicit for new team members
BashOperator chain — pass paths as command-line arguments through environment variables
EmptyOperator chain — push the paths as XCom keys directly from the EmptyOperator
questions.correctAnswer: TaskFlow @task — data passes between Python tasks; visible in the function signatures
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: when Python callables pass data, TaskFlow's signatures expose the data flow naturally. Classic XCom is verbose by comparison.

questions.question: In a mixed-style DAG, when should you reach for a classic operator instead of @task?
options: For BashOperator commands, EmptyOperator checkpoints, and provider operators — they have no TaskFlow equivalents
For any task that runs longer than five minutes — TaskFlow has a hard timeout cap on tasks
For any task that uses Variables — Variable.get() does not work inside @task functions
For any task that emits XCom — TaskFlow tasks have writable XCom only on success state
questions.correctAnswer: For BashOperator commands, EmptyOperator checkpoints, and provider operators — they have no TaskFlow equivalents
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: TaskFlow is for Python logic. BashOperator/EmptyOperator/provider operators stay classic. Mixing is the intended design.

questions.question: An engineer needs `requests==2.31.0` for one task that conflicts with the worker's installed version. Which decorator fits?
options: @task.virtualenv — runs the function in an isolated venv with pinned requirements
@task.bash — pip-installs requests inline before invoking the Python interpreter manually
@task.kubernetes — runs the function in a pod whose image includes the conflicting library
@task.docker — uses Docker layer caching to inject the requests library into the worker
questions.correctAnswer: @task.virtualenv — runs the function in an isolated venv with pinned requirements
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: @task.virtualenv installs the listed requirements in an isolated venv per execution — the right tool when library versions clash.

questions.question: SaulėGrid's rule: 'Use @task for new Python tasks; keep BashOperator and provider operators classic.' Why?
options: TaskFlow's value is in Python data passing — forcing it on bash and provider tasks loses fit
TaskFlow has stricter retry semantics that bash and provider operators cannot satisfy at all
Mixing within one DAG is forbidden by Airflow and the rule keeps every DAG TaskFlow-only
Provider operators only work with the legacy DAG context manager and not with TaskFlow
questions.correctAnswer: TaskFlow's value is in Python data passing — forcing it on bash and provider tasks loses fit
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: TaskFlow shines for Python data flow. Bash, Empty, and provider operators are best in their classic form.

questions.question: In SaulėGrid's TaskFlow Silver DAG, `flows = separate_flows(bronze)` is followed by `cons = aggregate_consumption(flows['consumption_path'])`. What is happening?
options: separate_flows returns multiple_outputs and each consumer picks the key it needs from the dict
separate_flows pushes a single XCom and aggregate_consumption pulls the entire blob each time
separate_flows triggers all consumers in parallel and ignores the data they need internally
separate_flows runs three sequential operators that each return a single value to consumers
questions.correctAnswer: separate_flows returns multiple_outputs and each consumer picks the key it needs from the dict
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: with multiple_outputs=True, the dict's keys become separate XComs. Different downstream tasks pull different keys.

questions.question: What does `download >> validate` express in classic operator syntax?
options: validate must happen after download — it does not necessarily mean data flows between them
download sends its return value to validate as a parameter via the XCom shared queue
download and validate are aliased — they refer to the same task instance under two names
validate replaces download in the metadata database after download has finished cleanly
questions.correctAnswer: validate must happen after download — it does not necessarily mean data flows between them
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: >> is 'must happen before', not 'sends data to'. In classic ops, dependency and data flow are independent.

questions.question: When is `chain()` the cleaner choice over `>>` for setting dependencies?
options: When the list is long, dynamically built, or needs cross-dependencies between two lists
When the dependency graph contains cycles that >> cannot express in a single expression
When using TaskFlow tasks because >> is reserved for classic operators only in Airflow
When the schedule is sub-hourly because >> evaluates dependencies once per scheduler tick
questions.correctAnswer: When the list is long, dynamically built, or needs cross-dependencies between two lists
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: chain() shines for long/dynamic chains and cross-dependencies between parallel lanes. Short chains stay readable with >>.

questions.question: What does `validate >> [aggregate_a, aggregate_b, aggregate_c]` create?
options: A fan-out: all three aggregations depend on validate and run in parallel after it succeeds
A fan-in: validate runs only after all three aggregations have completed in any order
A diamond: validate runs once before and once after the three parallel aggregations
A cycle: validate depends on the three aggregations which all depend on validate again
questions.correctAnswer: A fan-out: all three aggregations depend on validate and run in parallel after it succeeds
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: list on the right of >> = fan-out. The three children are independent of each other and run in parallel.

questions.question: What does `[aggregate_a, aggregate_b, aggregate_c] >> write_silver` enable as a quality gate?
options: write_silver runs only after all three aggregations succeed — a complete-or-nothing gate
write_silver runs as soon as the first aggregation succeeds without waiting for the others
write_silver runs three times — once per upstream — and writes whichever finishes first
write_silver runs on its own schedule and reads whatever results the aggregations have
questions.correctAnswer: write_silver runs only after all three aggregations succeed — a complete-or-nothing gate
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: fan-in synchronizes parallel work. Either all three aggregations make it into Silver, or none do.

questions.question: Module 6 lists five basic patterns from which every complex DAG is composed. Which set is correct?
options: Linear chain, fan-out, fan-in, diamond, and side branch
Cron, sensor, branch, retry, and rollback patterns across all DAGs
Bronze, Silver, Gold, regulatory, and billing patterns at SaulėGrid
Lambda, sidecar, broadcast, scatter-gather, and pipeline patterns
questions.correctAnswer: Linear chain, fan-out, fan-in, diamond, and side branch
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: every DAG is a composition of these five. Recognizing them in an existing DAG is faster than reading every edge individually.

questions.question: Where does the trigger_rule attribute belong in Airflow?
options: On the downstream task — one rule applies to all of its upstream dependencies at once
On each dependency edge — every >> can carry its own per-edge trigger rule individually
On the upstream task — its rule decides what state propagates to all of its downstream tasks
On the DAG itself — the rule applies uniformly to every task instance inside that DAG
questions.correctAnswer: On the downstream task — one rule applies to all of its upstream dependencies at once
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: trigger_rule is set on the downstream task. You cannot say 'tolerate failure on B but require A' — that needs branching, not a per-edge rule.

questions.question: After a BranchPythonOperator picks one branch, the rejoining task uses the default trigger_rule. What goes wrong?
options: The rejoin task is blocked because the unchosen branch is 'skipped' and all_success rejects skipped
The rejoin task runs twice — once for each branch — instead of consolidating the two paths
The rejoin task fails with a 'branch ambiguous' error because Airflow cannot pick a state
The rejoin task runs on the wrong branch because Airflow defaults to picking the first one
questions.correctAnswer: The rejoin task is blocked because the unchosen branch is 'skipped' and all_success rejects skipped
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: default trigger_rule is all_success, which treats skipped as not-success. Always use trigger_rule='none_failed' on the convergence task.

questions.question: What is the ShortCircuitOperator's behavior when its callable returns False?
options: All downstream tasks are skipped — like an early return at the start of a function
The DAG Run is marked failed and the scheduler stops creating new runs of this DAG
The downstream tasks run but with their states forced to 'failed' by Airflow's executor
The DAG Run pauses indefinitely until an operator manually clears the short-circuit task
questions.correctAnswer: All downstream tasks are skipped — like an early return at the start of a function
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: ShortCircuit returns True (continue) or False (skip everything downstream). Think of it as a guard clause for the pipeline.

questions.question: Why does SaulėGrid name dependency patterns when discussing DAG design?
options: Pattern names accelerate communication, design reviews, and onboarding for new engineers
Airflow requires a registered pattern name on every DAG before the scheduler will accept it
The metadata database indexes pattern names so the UI can group DAGs by structural type
Pattern names are exported as Prometheus metrics for the platform's reliability dashboards
questions.correctAnswer: Pattern names accelerate communication, design reviews, and onboarding for new engineers
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: 'Silver uses a diamond with a side branch for notifications' is faster than describing every edge. Pattern vocabulary is design speed.

questions.question: Module 6 closes by connecting dependencies to Module 7. What does each provide?
options: Dependencies define what runs after what; scheduling defines when each pipeline runs
Dependencies define how data flows; scheduling defines which engine processes the data
Dependencies define which executor runs each task; scheduling defines task retry counts
Dependencies define which DAGs the user can see; scheduling defines who can trigger them
questions.correctAnswer: Dependencies define what runs after what; scheduling defines when each pipeline runs
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: dependencies = order. Scheduling = time. Together they tell Airflow exactly what to run, in what order, at what time.

questions.question: What is the modern parameter name for setting how often a DAG runs?
options: schedule — replaces the deprecated schedule_interval and accepts cron, presets, or None
frequency — accepts seconds, minutes, hours, or days as integer values for cadence
trigger — combines schedule with manual trigger configuration in a single field
cron — accepts only cron expressions and rejects preset strings like @daily today
questions.correctAnswer: schedule — replaces the deprecated schedule_interval and accepts cron, presets, or None
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: schedule is the modern parameter. schedule_interval still works but logs a deprecation warning.

questions.question: What does the cron expression `0 */6 * * *` mean in plain English?
options: At minute 0, every 6th hour, any day, any month, any day of week — every 6 hours on the hour
At minute 0, hour 6, any day, any month, any day of week — once per day at exactly 06:00
At any minute, every 6th hour, any day, any month, any day of week — six times per hour
At minute 0, hour 6, on day 6 of the month, any month, any day of week — monthly on the 6th
questions.correctAnswer: At minute 0, every 6th hour, any day, any month, any day of week — every 6 hours on the hour
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: read aloud field by field. */6 in the hour position = every 6th hour. The schedule fires at 00:00, 06:00, 12:00, 18:00.

questions.question: Why does SaulėGrid prefer `@daily` over `0 0 * * *` when both are equivalent?
options: Communication — '@daily' immediately tells a reader the intent without parsing five fields
Performance — preset strings skip the cron parser and are evaluated faster every loop
Compatibility — older Airflow versions silently rejected '0 0 * * *' as malformed cron
Security — preset strings are sandboxed by Airflow while cron strings can run shell code
questions.correctAnswer: Communication — '@daily' immediately tells a reader the intent without parsing five fields
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: presets communicate intent. '@daily' is unmistakable; '0 0 * * *' requires either knowing cron or looking it up.

questions.question: A daily DAG triggers at midnight on March 23rd. Which date's data should the task process?
options: March 22nd — the DAG fires at the END of its data interval, processing the completed day
March 23rd — the DAG fires at the START of its data interval, processing the current day
March 24th — the DAG schedules ahead of time and processes tomorrow's data in advance
March 21st — the DAG fires two days behind to ensure all upstream sources are ready
questions.correctAnswer: March 22nd — the DAG fires at the END of its data interval, processing the completed day
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: Airflow fires at the END of the data interval. The midnight March 23 trigger processes March 22's completed data. Always use data_interval_start.

questions.question: Inside a task callable, why use `kwargs['ds']` (or `{{ ds }}` in BashOperator) instead of `datetime.now()`?
options: {{ ds }} returns the logical_date — the correct processing date even during retries or backfill
{{ ds }} is faster because it avoids the system call that datetime.now() makes per task
{{ ds }} is automatically timezone-corrected while datetime.now() always returns UTC
{{ ds }} is sandboxed by Airflow while datetime.now() can be tampered with by other DAGs
questions.correctAnswer: {{ ds }} returns the logical_date — the correct processing date even during retries or backfill
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: datetime.now() returns wall-clock time of execution — wrong during backfills/retries. {{ ds }} returns the logical processing date.

questions.question: An engineer deploys a new DAG with `start_date=2025-09-01` and the default `catchup=True`. What happens?
options: The scheduler creates a DAG Run for every missed interval since September — possibly hundreds
The scheduler creates a single DAG Run for the current date and ignores the historical dates
The scheduler refuses to deploy the DAG until the engineer sets a start_date in the future
The scheduler creates DAG Runs but throttles them to one per day to avoid overwhelming the system
questions.correctAnswer: The scheduler creates a DAG Run for every missed interval since September — possibly hundreds
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: catchup=True backfills every missed interval. Combined with a start_date months ago, the scheduler floods. Set max_active_runs=1.

questions.question: Why are manual DAG triggers a design feature, not a sign of failure?
options: They enable targeted reprocessing, testing, and ad-hoc analysis without rerunning the backlog
They are required because the scheduler stops working when the system clock drifts by a minute
They are the only way to run sub-hourly schedules since cron expressions are minute-bound
They bypass the metadata database which speeds up runtime by skipping state transitions
questions.correctAnswer: They enable targeted reprocessing, testing, and ad-hoc analysis without rerunning the backlog
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: scheduled runs handle routine, manual triggers handle exceptions. SaulėGrid uses them 2-3 times per week intentionally.

questions.question: An engineer writes `start_date=datetime(2026, 1, 1)` (no timezone). What does Airflow do?
options: Treats the naive datetime as UTC, silently shifting the schedule by 2-3 hours from local time
Raises an error at parse time because Airflow requires every datetime to carry a timezone
Treats the naive datetime as the local timezone of the scheduler host machine for the date
Treats the naive datetime as the user's browser timezone read from the web UI session cookie
questions.correctAnswer: Treats the naive datetime as UTC, silently shifting the schedule by 2-3 hours from local time
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: naive datetimes are treated as UTC. SaulėGrid requires pendulum.datetime(..., tz='Europe/Vilnius') for every start_date.

questions.question: When designing a schedule, where should the engineer start?
options: Work backward from the consumer's deadline — when does the business need the data ready?
Forward from the source — what time does the upstream SFTP file arrive each morning?
From the executor's idle window — schedule when worker capacity is otherwise unused
From a default 6 AM time — every team eventually converges on early-morning schedules
questions.correctAnswer: Work backward from the consumer's deadline — when does the business need the data ready?
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: every schedule pattern maps to a business requirement. Start with 'when does the consumer need the data?' and work backward to a schedule.

questions.question: Module 7 closes by previewing Module 8. Which trio does Module 8 introduce?
options: Connections (credentials), Hooks (Python wrappers), and Variables (configuration)
Datasets (events), Triggers (asyncio), and Deferrable Operators (resource-light waits)
Sensors (polling), Pools (rate limits), and Slots (executor capacity allocation)
questions.correctAnswer: Connections (credentials), Hooks (Python wrappers), and Variables (configuration)
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: Module 8 covers how Airflow integrates with external systems — Connections, Hooks, Variables.

questions.question: Why do Connections exist as a separate concept from DAG code?
options: DAG code references a connection ID; the credentials live in the metadata DB or env vars
DAG code embeds credentials directly so each pipeline owns its security context cleanly
DAG code reads credentials from the worker's filesystem at runtime via the Hook layer
DAG code negotiates credentials per-task with the executor over a secure local channel
questions.correctAnswer: DAG code references a connection ID; the credentials live in the metadata DB or env vars
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: Connections separate the address (DAG references 'sftp_saulegrid_meters') from the credentials (stored in DB). Rotation = update one record.

questions.question: An engineer hardcodes `'sftp://etl:S3cur3@sftp.saulegrid.lt:22'` in a DAG file. What is wrong?
options: Credentials in DAG code are a security violation — DAGs reference connection IDs only
Hardcoded URIs bypass the metadata database and slow the executor's auth handshake
URI strings are case-sensitive in Airflow and small typos cause silent connection failures
Hardcoded connections do not auto-reconnect when the SFTP server rotates its host key
questions.correctAnswer: Credentials in DAG code are a security violation — DAGs reference connection IDs only
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: SaulėGrid's code review blocks any PR with credential-like strings in DAGs. The DAG knows the ID; the metadata knows the secret.

questions.question: An engineer wants to add an Amazon S3 connection but it is not in the UI dropdown. Why?
options: The S3 provider package (apache-airflow-providers-amazon) is not installed on this host
S3 connections must be created via the CLI because the UI form requires extra fields
S3 is supported only in commercial Airflow distributions and not in the open-source build
S3 connections are auto-created from environment variables and never appear in the dropdown
questions.correctAnswer: The S3 provider package (apache-airflow-providers-amazon) is not installed on this host
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: out-of-the-box: SFTP, Postgres, HTTP. Anything else (S3, BigQuery, Snowflake) requires installing the provider package first.

questions.question: What is a Hook's role in Airflow's external-system integration?
options: A Python wrapper that resolves a Connection at runtime and provides methods to interact
A scheduler hook that fires before each task to validate the executor has free capacity
A web-server hook that decorates the UI with platform-specific badges per Connection type
A pre-commit hook that scans DAG files for credential leaks during developer commits
questions.correctAnswer: A Python wrapper that resolves a Connection at runtime and provides methods to interact
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: Hook = Python class that wraps a Connection. PostgresHook.get_conn() returns a psycopg2 connection authenticated with the Connection's credentials.

questions.question: Why is `Variable.get('quality_threshold')` at the top level of a DAG file dangerous?
options: It queries the metadata database on every scheduler parse — many queries per minute total
It locks the variable for that DAG and prevents other DAGs from reading the same value
It caches the value forever and ignores any updates the operator makes through the UI
It crashes the scheduler when the variable is missing instead of using a default value
questions.correctAnswer: It queries the metadata database on every scheduler parse — many queries per minute total
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: top-level code runs on every parse. Variable.get() at module level = constant DB hammering. Move it inside task callables.

questions.question: Which practice does SaulėGrid use to enforce 'no credentials in DAG code'?
options: A pre-commit hook that scans for credential-like patterns and blocks the commit
Manual review by a senior engineer for every PR with a 'security' label attached
A weekly audit that grep-searches the repository for hardcoded passwords retroactively
A runtime executor check that crashes any task whose source contains credential strings
questions.correctAnswer: A pre-commit hook that scans for credential-like patterns and blocks the commit
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: discipline lapses; automation does not. Pre-commit hooks catch the moments when 'just for testing' would otherwise slip through.

questions.question: What does installing `apache-airflow-providers-postgres` give you?
options: PostgresOperator, PostgresHook, and a 'Postgres' connection type in the UI dropdown
Direct access to PostgreSQL's internal query planner from inside Airflow task code
An embedded PostgreSQL database that Airflow can use as its own metadata backend
A migration tool that converts existing MySQL connections to PostgreSQL connections automatically
questions.correctAnswer: PostgresOperator, PostgresHook, and a 'Postgres' connection type in the UI dropdown
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: providers add operators + hooks + connection types for one platform. Postgres provider = PostgresOperator + PostgresHook + 'Postgres' UI type.

questions.question: Lesson 8 lists three steps for integrating any new platform. What are they?
options: Install the provider package, create the Connection, use the operator referencing the connection ID
Write a custom Python wrapper, configure the executor, register the wrapper as a plugin module
Write a Bash script, schedule it with cron, and trust Airflow to discover it on next parse cycle
Subclass BaseOperator, implement the execute method, and add the new operator to the DAG file
questions.correctAnswer: Install the provider package, create the Connection, use the operator referencing the connection ID
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: pip install provider → airflow connections add → use operator(conn_id='...'). Same three steps for Postgres, S3, Snowflake, Slack.

questions.question: Why does SaulėGrid rename a Connection to `deprecated_<id>` before deleting it?
options: Renaming surfaces missed references on the next scheduler cycle before deletion is final
Renaming archives the credential history and is required by Lithuanian regulators legally
Renaming forces Airflow to roll the password and protects against credential reuse attacks
Renaming creates a snapshot that the scheduler caches for 90 days as a recovery option
questions.correctAnswer: Renaming surfaces missed references on the next scheduler cycle before deletion is final
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: deletion is immediate with no grace period. Renaming first lets a full schedule cycle catch any missed reference before final deletion.

questions.question: Lesson 10's integration architecture separates four layers. Which list matches?
options: Connections (credentials), Hooks (methods), Operators (tasks), and Variables (configuration)
Datasets (events), Triggers (asyncio), Deferrable Operators (waits), and Sensors (polls)
DAGs (code), Pools (slots), Queues (priorities), and Workers (executors)
Schedulers (decisions), Executors (work), Web servers (visibility), and Databases (state)
questions.correctAnswer: Connections (credentials), Hooks (methods), Operators (tasks), and Variables (configuration)
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: Connections + Hooks + Operators + Variables = the four-layer integration model. Each layer is independently swappable.

questions.question: In the Airflow web UI, which section does an engineer spend roughly 90% of their time in?
options: DAGs — the list, grid, graph, and logs for daily monitoring and incident response
Admin — Connections, Variables, Pools, and provider configuration management
Browse — cross-DAG search, DAG dependencies, audit logs, and task instance lookup
Security — user roles, permissions, RBAC configuration, and access control lists
questions.correctAnswer: DAGs — the list, grid, graph, and logs for daily monitoring and incident response
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: DAGs is where monitoring, debugging, and triggering happen. Admin/Browse/Security are occasional visits.

questions.question: What is the DAG List view's job in SaulėGrid's morning routine?
options: Identify which DAGs need diagnosis — not diagnose them. Red circles say 'needs attention'.
Display the latest task log line for each DAG in a single scrollable view across DAGs.
Render the dependency graph for every DAG inline so the operator can scan all at once.
Show every Connection's status with green/red indicators across the full integration matrix.
questions.correctAnswer: Identify which DAGs need diagnosis — not diagnose them. Red circles say 'needs attention'.
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: the list view answers 'which DAGs, if any, need investigation?' Diagnosis happens in Grid View and logs.

questions.question: What does the Grid View tell you that the DAG List view does not?
options: Which task on which date failed, and whether downstream tasks were skipped or completed
Which executor slot ran each task and how much CPU each task consumed during execution
Which DAG file changed most recently and which engineer authored the most recent commit
Which Connection each task used and whether the credential was rotated within 90 days
questions.correctAnswer: Which task on which date failed, and whether downstream tasks were skipped or completed
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: Grid View narrows from 'this DAG failed' to 'this specific task on this specific date failed' typically in under 10 seconds.

questions.question: When is the Graph View the right tool to use?
options: After every DAG deployment — verify the dependency structure matches the design visually
After every task failure — read the task logs inside the Graph View's right-hand panel
After every schedule change — verify the cron expression matches the intended cadence
After every Connection rotation — verify the new credentials propagated across worker hosts
questions.correctAnswer: After every DAG deployment — verify the dependency structure matches the design visually
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: Graph View = visual code review of dependencies. A missing >> shows up as a missing edge — visually obvious in a way the source might not be.

questions.question: An engineer clicks 'Mark Success' on a failed task. What happens?
options: The task's database state changes to success — the task code is NOT actually executed
Airflow re-runs the task from scratch and updates the database when it completes cleanly
Airflow rolls back to the last successful run and discards the failed task instance entirely
Airflow runs only the failed lines of code in the task and leaves successful steps unchanged
questions.correctAnswer: The task's database state changes to success — the task code is NOT actually executed
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: Mark Success only changes the DB state. Use it only when you have already done the work through other means (manual upload, etc.).

questions.question: What does SaulėGrid's logging convention require a task log to answer?
options: What was the task trying to do, what happened during execution, and why did it fail or succeed
Which executor ran the task, how many seconds it took, and what its memory peak was
Which engineer last modified the DAG file, what their email is, and when the file was committed
Which Connection it used, what the password expiration date is, and which provider it called
questions.correctAnswer: What was the task trying to do, what happened during execution, and why did it fail or succeed
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: logs are read at 8 AM by someone who may not know the task. Three questions answered = a good log.

questions.question: What is the Gantt View specifically useful for at SaulėGrid?
options: Performance analysis — how long each task took and where time was spent in the run
Dependency analysis — the graph structure of upstream and downstream task relationships
Schedule analysis — the cron expression's effect on the cadence of historical DAG Runs
Connection analysis — the latency of each external system across the run's lifecycle
questions.correctAnswer: Performance analysis — how long each task took and where time was spent in the run
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: Gantt is the performance tool. Grid/Graph diagnose; Gantt times. Bar lengths reveal bottlenecks; positions reveal parallelism.

questions.question: What is the Calendar View's primary purpose?
options: Reveal trends — are we getting more or less reliable? Which DAGs have the most red days?
Show the schedule's next-fire times for the upcoming month across every DAG in the system
Display every Connection rotation date so engineers can plan credential refreshes ahead
Render every executor's free slots over time to inform pool capacity planning decisions
questions.correctAnswer: Reveal trends — are we getting more or less reliable? Which DAGs have the most red days?
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: Calendar turns individual incidents into a reliability narrative — the trend that guides infrastructure investment decisions.

questions.question: What is SaulėGrid's five-step troubleshooting workflow?
options: Identify (DAG List) → Locate (Grid) → Inspect (Logs) → Diagnose → Remediate
Restart → Retry → Reset → Refresh → Redeploy until the failed task succeeds
Page → Triage → Escalate → Investigate → Document the incident in the postmortem
Pause → Backfill → Unpause → Validate → Notify the consumer of the new data
questions.correctAnswer: Identify (DAG List) → Locate (Grid) → Inspect (Logs) → Diagnose → Remediate
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: a junior who follows the workflow diagnoses as effectively as a senior 'who just knows where to look' — the workflow ensures the right views in the right order.

questions.question: How long is SaulėGrid's daily morning health check when everything is green?
options: About two minutes — open DAGs page, scan for red, filter by 'bronze' and 'gold', done
About thirty minutes — read every task log line by line for every overnight DAG run
About one hour — review the Calendar, Gantt, and Graph views for every production DAG
About fifteen seconds — a Slack bot pages only when something is red so no UI visit is needed
questions.correctAnswer: About two minutes — open DAGs page, scan for red, filter by 'bronze' and 'gold', done
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: morning routine is fast because the UI is designed for visual status checks. Two minutes when green; up to fifteen on a failure.

questions.question: Lesson 1 makes a key claim about the design process for production DAGs. What is it?
options: Building a production DAG is assembly of known concepts, not invention of new ones
Building a production DAG requires inventing custom operators that the team owns long-term
Building a production DAG starts with a UML diagram that the team approves before any code
Building a production DAG begins with the Connection layer and proceeds outward to schedules
questions.correctAnswer: Building a production DAG is assembly of known concepts, not invention of new ones
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: schedule from Module 7, dependencies from 6, operators from 4, retries from 4, Connections from 8. Composition of what you already know.

questions.question: SaulėGrid's production Bronze DAG uses three operator styles together. Which combination?
options: TaskFlow @task for Python logic, BashOperator for the SFTP script, EmptyOperator for sentinels
Only PythonOperator for everything because uniform style is easier for new engineers to read
Only TaskFlow @task because classic operators are deprecated in modern Airflow versions
Only BashOperator because Bronze ingestion is fundamentally a shell-script concern at scale
questions.correctAnswer: TaskFlow @task for Python logic, BashOperator for the SFTP script, EmptyOperator for sentinels
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: TaskFlow for Python data passing, BashOperator for the existing SFTP script, EmptyOperator for start/end sentinels. Mixing is intentional.

questions.question: SaulėGrid's Silver DAG uses TaskFlow for fan-out + fan-in. Why TaskFlow over classic operators here?
options: The diamond pattern emerges visibly from function signatures — fan-out and fan-in by reading the DAG
TaskFlow is the only style that can express fan-out and fan-in patterns in modern Airflow
TaskFlow auto-tunes parallelism better than classic operators when the executor is LocalExecutor
TaskFlow stores XCom in a separate database that scales linearly with the number of branches
questions.correctAnswer: The diamond pattern emerges visibly from function signatures — fan-out and fan-in by reading the DAG
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: in TaskFlow, three calls reading three keys = visible fan-out. One function consuming three return values = visible fan-in. Code IS the diagram.

questions.question: SaulėGrid's Gold DAG uses BranchPythonOperator. What does the branch decide?
options: Whether to include the regulatory report on business days or skip it on weekends
Whether to use TaskFlow or classic operators based on the runtime configuration values
Which Connection ID the downstream PostgresOperator should use for the warehouse insert
Which executor slot the downstream tasks should run in based on current pool utilization
questions.correctAnswer: Whether to include the regulatory report on business days or skip it on weekends
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: business logic that varies by day. Mon-Fri = include regulatory; weekends = skip. The rejoining task uses trigger_rule='none_failed'.

questions.question: Why does SaulėGrid limit ExternalTaskSensor usage to a small number per Airflow installation?
options: Each sensor occupies an executor slot for its full wait — many sensors exhaust parallelism
Each sensor opens a TCP connection to the metadata database that never closes correctly
Each sensor crashes the scheduler when its timeout fires before the upstream DAG finishes
Each sensor doubles the storage cost of the metadata database for the duration of the wait
questions.correctAnswer: Each sensor occupies an executor slot for its full wait — many sensors exhaust parallelism
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: sensors hold slots. SaulėGrid's three sensors are fine. Fifty waiting simultaneously could exhaust executor parallelism. Datasets are the slot-free alternative.

questions.question: Lesson 6 reframes Airflow error handling. What is its core insight?
options: Error handling is configuring the orchestration layer (retries, deps, callbacks) — not try/except
Error handling is wrapping every callable in try/except blocks that swallow and log failures
Error handling is post-hoc — write a separate cleanup DAG that runs after every failure
Error handling is delegated to the executor — Python code should never catch any exception
questions.correctAnswer: Error handling is configuring the orchestration layer (retries, deps, callbacks) — not try/except
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: retries + dependency protection + on_failure_callback + all_done notify = architectural decisions that protect the pipeline. Try/except is the wrong layer.

questions.question: What three questions should a SaulėGrid task log answer for the on-call engineer at 8 AM?
options: What was the task trying to do, what happened during execution, and why did it fail or succeed
Which engineer wrote the code, when did they commit, and what their email address is
Which executor ran the task, how much memory it used, and how many CPU cores it took
Which version of Airflow ran it, which provider package it used, and which UI version rendered it
questions.correctAnswer: What was the task trying to do, what happened during execution, and why did it fail or succeed
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: at 8 AM the reader may not know the code. Logs that answer the three questions are good logs.

questions.question: How does SaulėGrid treat DAG files at the operational level?
options: As production code — version control, code review, testing, and documented deployment
As scratch files — engineers can edit them in production without review and redeploy fast
As config files — they are managed by ops separately from any application code
As scheduled scripts — they run via cron and bypass the standard CI/CD pipeline
questions.correctAnswer: As production code — version control, code review, testing, and documented deployment
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: DAG files run nightly with real consequences (billing, regulatory). They get the same rigor as PySpark transformation scripts.

questions.question: What does Lesson 9 say about performance tuning at junior scale?
options: Avoid common mistakes — keep DAG files lightweight, set timeouts, fan out independent tasks
Tune the scheduler's parsing interval to one second to maximize scheduler throughput
Run every DAG with KubernetesExecutor for maximum process isolation and parallelism
Pre-warm the executor on each parse cycle by making it run a tiny synthetic task on demand
questions.correctAnswer: Avoid common mistakes — keep DAG files lightweight, set timeouts, fan out independent tasks
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: at junior scale, performance is about avoiding mistakes — heavy imports, Variable.get() at module level, missing timeouts. Tuning comes later.

questions.question: Lesson 10 closes the junior track. Which of these reflects what the learner now has?
options: A complete toolkit — DAGs, tasks, dependencies, schedules, connections, monitoring
A custom operator framework — the ability to write any operator from scratch in a day
A production deployment plan — Kubernetes, Helm, monitoring stacks, alerting routes
A migration plan — moving every existing cron job to Airflow within a single quarter
questions.correctAnswer: A complete toolkit — DAGs, tasks, dependencies, schedules, connections, monitoring
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: the foundation is complete. Whether the next step is mid-level track, production Airflow, or your own project — the toolkit is there.

questions.question: SaulėGrid's 12 cron-based DAGs use 90-minute time buffers between layers. What is the fundamental flaw in this scheduling approach?
options: The 'when' is encoded as a timing assumption rather than as an explicit data dependency
Cron expressions cannot express sub-hour schedules so the chain has unavoidable lag
The scheduler cannot run more than three cron-scheduled DAGs in the same five-minute window
Cron silently shifts every schedule by exactly one minute on each daylight-savings transition
questions.correctAnswer: The 'when' is encoded as a timing assumption rather than as an explicit data dependency
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: time buffers assume processing duration is predictable. When Bronze runs 40 minutes late, every downstream DAG processes incomplete data because the scheduler never sees the dependency.

questions.question: What is an Airflow Dataset, and how do producers and consumers connect through one?
options: A logical URI that producing tasks declare via outlets and consuming DAGs reference via schedule
A physical S3 bucket that Airflow creates automatically when the first producer task is registered
An on-disk Parquet file Airflow writes after each task and reads back into the next task's executor
A scheduler-side cache of the most recent task return value tagged with a producer DAG identifier
questions.correctAnswer: A logical URI that producing tasks declare via outlets and consuming DAGs reference via schedule
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: Dataset is just a URI. Producers say outlets=[ds], consumers say schedule=[ds]. The scheduler tracks the link between them.

questions.question: If a Gold DAG has `schedule=[silver_meters, silver_generation]`, when does it trigger?
options: When BOTH datasets are updated — the scheduler waits for AND across all listed datasets
When EITHER dataset is updated — the scheduler triggers on the first update it observes
When the FIRST dataset listed is updated — the second is documentation for human readers only
When MOST datasets are updated — the scheduler accepts a quorum of two-thirds to fire the run
questions.correctAnswer: When BOTH datasets are updated — the scheduler waits for AND across all listed datasets
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: a list of datasets in schedule= is AND logic. Gold billing waits for both Silver meters AND Silver generation before triggering.

questions.question: Lesson 4 says Dataset chains 'fail silently by design.' What does this mean operationally?
options: A consumer that is never triggered has no failed run to alert on — needs freshness monitoring
A consumer that fails posts no error message to the Airflow UI for the operator to investigate
A consumer that succeeds writes no audit log entry that the security team can later review
A consumer that retries silently succeeds on the second attempt without telling the operator
questions.correctAnswer: A consumer that is never triggered has no failed run to alert on — needs freshness monitoring
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: an upstream that didn't update means the downstream DAG was never created. Pair Datasets with a freshness-monitoring DAG.

questions.question: When should you use a custom Timetable instead of cron + branching to skip holidays?
options: When the DAG should not have a DAG Run on holidays — no metadata record, no UI entry, no overhead
When the DAG should branch on holidays — the holiday logic must be visible in the Graph View
When the DAG must wait for a Dataset on holidays — the timetable handles cross-DAG dependencies
When the DAG ID must change on holidays — the timetable assigns a holiday suffix to the run ID
questions.correctAnswer: When the DAG should not have a DAG Run on holidays — no metadata record, no UI entry, no overhead
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: custom timetable = no run is created. Branching = run is created and tasks are skipped. SaulėGrid's regulatory DAG uses a timetable to keep the audit trail clean.

questions.question: Which built-in timetable matches 'every 4 hours starting from the start_date'?
options: DeltaDataIntervalTimetable(interval=duration(hours=4))
EventsTimetable(events=[every_four_hours])
CronTriggerTimetable(cron='0 */4 * * *', interval='dataset')
DatasetOrTimeSchedule(timetable=None, hours=4)
questions.correctAnswer: DeltaDataIntervalTimetable(interval=duration(hours=4))
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: DeltaDataIntervalTimetable runs every fixed interval. CronTrigger uses cron strings; Events uses a list of explicit dates.

questions.question: An SLA-critical Gold DAG combines Datasets and a 06:00 cron. Which combination keeps the data ready by 06:00 even if Silver is late?
options: DatasetOrTimeSchedule with OR — fires on the first signal: Dataset update OR 06:00 cron
DatasetAndTimeSchedule with AND — fires only when both Silver is fresh AND it is past 06:00
CronTriggerTimetable alone — Datasets are too unreliable for SLA-critical Gold pipelines
EventsTimetable with both signals — fires once per signal regardless of which arrives first
questions.correctAnswer: DatasetOrTimeSchedule with OR — fires on the first signal: Dataset update OR 06:00 cron
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: OR guarantees a run by 06:00 even if Silver is late. AND would skip the run entirely on the morning Silver is delayed — exactly when billing needs to run.

questions.question: SaulėGrid has an event-triggered DAG `dag_bronze_reprocess`. Which configuration is correct for this kind of DAG?
options: schedule=None, params with sensible defaults, and a validation task as the first step
schedule='@once', params=None, and a validation task running last as a final check
schedule='@daily' with catchup=False so it auto-runs but accepts external triggers too
schedule=ExternalTaskSensor(...) so the DAG waits for the upstream API call to finish
questions.correctAnswer: schedule=None, params with sensible defaults, and a validation task as the first step
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: schedule=None means 'no automatic schedule'. params with defaults make manual triggers easy. First-task validation prevents bad input from reaching processing.

questions.question: Why are ExternalTaskSensors a known anti-pattern at scale?
options: Each sensor occupies a worker slot during its wait — many sensors exhaust the worker pool
