As config-only changes — modify the schema freely; the factory's tests catch any regressions early
questions.correctAnswer: As API changes — backward compatibility, clear errors, progressive disclosure, version the schema
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: the schema is the factory's API surface. Pipeline teams should not rewrite configs when the factory is upgraded. Schema = API.

questions.question: Why does BalticSolar log the resolved configuration with each field's source (base/country/domain/pipeline)?
options: Debugging a template hierarchy at 2 AM requires tracing which template provided each value — manual reading is error-prone
Logging the source layer is required by the regulator's audit framework for any factory-generated DAG
The Airflow scheduler refuses to register a DAG whose config did not log its layer source on parse
Layer attribution lets the factory throttle parse time by skipping unchanged layers between heartbeats
questions.correctAnswer: Debugging a template hierarchy at 2 AM requires tracing which template provided each value — manual reading is error-prone
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: four-layer merge is hard to debug without traceability. Logging field-by-field source makes incident response much faster.

questions.question: What is the key constraint of dynamic task generation in the factory?
options: Tasks are created at PARSE time, not execution — adding a source requires re-parsing before the new task appears
Tasks are created at EXECUTION time so the scheduler must observe a full DagRun to see the task graph
Tasks are created at SCHEDULER startup so the platform must restart whenever the YAML config is changed
questions.correctAnswer: Tasks are created at PARSE time, not execution — adding a source requires re-parsing before the new task appears
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: parse-time generation is fundamental to Airflow's architecture. The factory reads the config during parse; task count cannot change between DagRuns without re-parsing.

questions.question: What is the paradigm shift in factory testing compared to hand-crafted DAG testing?
options: The factory code is the unit under test, not the generated DAGs — config validation + DAG integrity + snapshots
Each generated DAG has its own test file that runs the standard DAG integrity checks per pipeline
Generated DAGs cannot be tested directly because their structure is determined at runtime by config
Factory testing replaces unit tests with end-to-end tests because the factory's logic is too complex
questions.correctAnswer: The factory code is the unit under test, not the generated DAGs — config validation + DAG integrity + snapshots
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: O(1) test maintenance for 180 DAGs. Test the factory's input boundary, output correctness, and snapshot stability.

questions.question: What does the factory's MAJOR/MINOR/PATCH versioning communicate to pipeline teams?
options: MAJOR = config may need modification; MINOR = new capability available; PATCH = bug fix, no impact
MAJOR = team must rebuild from scratch; MINOR = team must rerun migration; PATCH = team is unaffected
MAJOR = scheduler restart required; MINOR = factory restart required; PATCH = no restart required at all
MAJOR = all configs invalidated; MINOR = new schema fields default to null; PATCH = output unchanged
questions.correctAnswer: MAJOR = config may need modification; MINOR = new capability available; PATCH = bug fix, no impact
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: semantic versioning communicates impact. Version is embedded in every generated DAG's tags so the team can track which factory built each DAG.

questions.question: BalticSolar's factory took 6 weeks to build but 18 months to migrate every team to it. Why?
options: Organizational change (trust, governance, exceptions) takes 3× longer than the technical implementation
Pipeline teams refused to migrate without a Lithuanian regulatory exemption granted on a per-DAG basis
Airflow's parser performance dropped during the migration so each new factory DAG required tuning
The metadata database was rebuilt three times during the migration as the schema kept changing
questions.correctAnswer: Organizational change (trust, governance, exceptions) takes 3× longer than the technical implementation
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: technical architects underestimate organizational change. Budget 3× more time for org change than for tech work.

questions.question: What protects a factory's simplicity over time?
options: Discipline about exceptions — every pipeline that bypasses the factory with documented justification keeps it simple
Allowing the factory to absorb every edge case so all 200 DAGs go through one unified code path
Forking the factory into per-team variants so each team's edge cases live in their own version
Locking the schema after launch so no new fields are added regardless of team requirements
questions.correctAnswer: Discipline about exceptions — every pipeline that bypasses the factory with documented justification keeps it simple
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: a factory that absorbs every edge case grows until its complexity exceeds the cost of hand-crafted DAGs. Disciplined exceptions are a feature.

questions.question: What is the long-term architectural value of the DAG factory beyond maintenance reduction?
options: Distribution mechanism — every platform improvement is automatically available to all 180 generated DAGs
Cost reduction — the factory uses 40% fewer scheduler resources than hand-crafted DAGs at the same scale
Multi-tenancy — the factory is the only Airflow component that supports multi-tenant deployments
Migration path — the factory automatically rewrites configs when Airflow major versions are released
questions.correctAnswer: Distribution mechanism — every platform improvement is automatically available to all 180 generated DAGs
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: the factory is the leverage. Every Module 3-9 improvement (scheduling, executors, integration, observability) propagates to 180 DAGs without team action.

questions.question: How does scheduling change conceptually between 12 DAGs and 200+ DAGs?
options: From a configuration problem (set cron, set trigger) to a resource allocation problem with governance
From a Python problem (write the DAG) to a YAML problem (configure the schedule for each DAG)
From a single-machine problem (one scheduler) to a multi-region problem (scheduler per data center)
From a deterministic problem (fixed cron) to a non-deterministic problem (event-driven only)
questions.correctAnswer: From a configuration problem (set cron, set trigger) to a resource allocation problem with governance
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: at scale, scheduling is finite slots, competing priorities, regulatory deadlines, cascading deps — a governance framework, not a config dialog.

questions.question: Why does BalticSolar centralize external deadlines (Nord Pool, ENTSO-E, VERT) in a timezone registry?
options: DAGs reference logical deadlines; the registry handles DST conversion so DAGs do not embed UTC offsets
The registry caches deadlines in memory so the scheduler avoids re-reading them on every heartbeat
The registry is required by Lithuanian regulation because each external deadline must have an audit owner
The registry encrypts deadlines so external system schedules are not exposed via the Airflow audit log
questions.correctAnswer: DAGs reference logical deadlines; the registry handles DST conversion so DAGs do not embed UTC offsets
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: DST shifts UTC offsets twice a year. Hardcoding 06:00 UTC in DAGs breaks during transitions. The registry centralizes the conversion.

questions.question: Why does BalticSolar's quarterly review challenge teams whose DAGs exceed 15% platinum?
options: Priority is only meaningful when scarce — over-allocation makes the platinum designation meaningless
Lithuanian regulations cap the number of platinum-tier DAGs that any single team can operate at once
The scheduler refuses to dispatch more than 15% platinum tasks per heartbeat regardless of pool slots
The platform team charges 4× the standard per-task cost for any DAG above the 15% platinum threshold
questions.correctAnswer: Priority is only meaningful when scarce — over-allocation makes the platinum designation meaningless
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: target distribution 10/25/50/15. If 80% are platinum, the other 20% starve and platinum loses meaning. Reviews catch 30-40% mis-tagged.

questions.question: What is a 'maintenance window' in BalticSolar's scheduling calendar?
options: A contract — the platform team guarantees infra changes only inside it; pipeline teams keep DAGs out of it
A flexible target — engineers prefer to do maintenance inside it but may deviate when pressed for time
A deployment freeze — no DAG deployments are allowed during the window across the entire alliance
A monitoring blackout — alerts are suppressed during the window so the on-call engineer is not paged
questions.correctAnswer: A contract — the platform team guarantees infra changes only inside it; pipeline teams keep DAGs out of it
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: contract between platform and pipeline teams. Breaking either side damages trust. The calendar formalizes the agreement so both can verify compliance.

questions.question: Why does BalticSolar use conditional Dataset updates instead of a simple `outlets=[...]` declaration?
options: Update the outlet only if data passes quality checks — bad data quarantined, consumers not triggered
Update the outlet only if the run is the first of the day — duplicate updates from retries are dropped
Update the outlet only if the producing task ran on the platinum pool — silver tasks do not trigger consumers
Update the outlet only if the consumer is currently idle — busy consumers ignore upstream updates entirely
questions.correctAnswer: Update the outlet only if data passes quality checks — bad data quarantined, consumers not triggered
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: a Dataset says 'data is ready', not 'data is good'. Conditional updates make the signal meaningful — quality-checked data triggers consumers.

questions.question: Why must a custom Timetable's `next_dagrun_info()` be a pure function?
options: The scheduler may call it multiple times per heartbeat from multiple processes in any order
The scheduler must serialize and replay timetable calls during HA failover to avoid double-creates
Pure functions are required by the Airflow community style guide for any timetable plugin contribution
Pure functions enable the scheduler to cache the next DagRun in Redis to skip future timetable calls
questions.correctAnswer: The scheduler may call it multiple times per heartbeat from multiple processes in any order
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: same inputs must return same outputs and not modify state. DB reads, API calls, instance variable mutations are sources of subtle bugs.

questions.question: Why does BalticSolar prefer prevention over resolution for scheduling conflicts?
options: Prevention (pool reservation, staggered scheduling, fairness caps) reduces the conflicts the scheduler must resolve
Resolution at the scheduler is forbidden by Airflow's design and engineers must always work around the constraint
Prevention is required by the regulator because every scheduling conflict triggers a compliance review
Prevention removes the need for priority weights so all DAGs share equal access to the executor pool
questions.correctAnswer: Prevention (pool reservation, staggered scheduling, fairness caps) reduces the conflicts the scheduler must resolve
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: pool reservation prevents tier-mixing; staggered scheduling reduces peak contention 60%; fairness caps prevent monopolization. Fewer conflicts = more predictable.

questions.question: Why must a backfill run differ behaviorally from a production run for the same DAG?
options: Skip notifications, skip SLA checks, skip cross-instance triggers, write to backfill paths — historical data is always 'late'
Run on the platinum pool — backfills are critical because they fill gaps in the regulatory audit trail
Run twice — once to validate the data, once to commit it — to account for the historical context loss
Use the live data sources — backfills must always use the current state of upstream systems for accuracy
questions.correctAnswer: Skip notifications, skip SLA checks, skip cross-instance triggers, write to backfill paths — historical data is always 'late'
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: an `is_backfill` flag enables behavioral differences. Same DAG code; different runtime behavior. Avoids 90 days of trigger spam to the coordinator.

questions.question: Lesson 9 says scheduling anti-patterns 'are not wrong at 12 DAGs.' What is the platform team's responsibility?
options: Detect when the platform approaches each anti-pattern's threshold and apply the architectural fix early
Forbid the anti-patterns globally so no team can introduce them regardless of their current scale
Wait for production incidents to surface anti-patterns and fix them reactively as they cause damage
Force every team to adopt the senior architecture from day one regardless of their actual scale or needs
questions.correctAnswer: Detect when the platform approaches each anti-pattern's threshold and apply the architectural fix early
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: each anti-pattern has a different threshold. Platform team monitors approach and applies the fix before incidents — not preemptively, not too late.

questions.question: What does BalticSolar's weekly scheduling review prevent?
options: Gradual accumulation of anti-patterns (priority inflation, over-pooling, herds) until an incident forces redesign
Cron expression typos that would otherwise produce DAGs scheduled at impossible times like 32:00 daily
Database bloat in the metadata database that would otherwise consume more than the allocated 5GB capacity
Plug-in conflicts between DAG factories owned by different teams that share the same Airflow instance
questions.correctAnswer: Gradual accumulation of anti-patterns (priority inflation, over-pooling, herds) until an incident forces redesign
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: scheduling architecture is not built once. The weekly review adapts it to changing DAG counts, priorities, deadlines, and capacity.

questions.question: What does the executor selection determine for the platform?
options: The runtime contract with each task — startup latency, isolation, resource sharing, failure modes
The Airflow version compatible with the platform — Celery for 2.x, Kubernetes for 3.x
The team's permission model — Celery operators get full RBAC; Kubernetes operators get limited RBAC
The DAG file structure — Celery DAGs use TaskFlow; Kubernetes DAGs must use classic operators only
questions.correctAnswer: The runtime contract with each task — startup latency, isolation, resource sharing, failure modes
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: Celery promises low-latency startup but shared resources. K8s promises isolation but startup overhead. Hybrid promises both at higher complexity.

questions.question: Why does BalticSolar monitor etcd write latency on the Kubernetes cluster running KubernetesExecutor?
options: Pod churn drives etcd writes — high P99 latency is a leading indicator of pod scaling limits being hit
etcd is the secrets store and high latency means tasks cannot resolve credentials before timing out
etcd holds the DAG file index and slow writes block the scheduler's parser from registering new DAGs
etcd stores the XCom payloads and high latency causes downstream tasks to read stale upstream output
questions.correctAnswer: Pod churn drives etcd writes — high P99 latency is a leading indicator of pod scaling limits being hit
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: every pod create/delete is an etcd write. 150 pods/day = 1000+ writes. > 50ms P99 = approaching the cluster's churn ceiling.

questions.question: Why is Redis identified as the single point of failure in CeleryExecutor?
options: If Redis is down, no tasks can be dispatched and no results reported — the broker is the only path
If Redis is down, the metadata database loses connectivity because they share a connection pool
If Redis is down, every running task is killed because Celery workers heartbeat through Redis only
questions.correctAnswer: If Redis is down, no tasks can be dispatched and no results reported — the broker is the only path
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: broker outage stalls the entire pipeline. Redis Sentinel + 60-second alert mitigates. Brief outages don't lose state (DB has it); sustained ones halt scheduling.

questions.question: Why does BalticSolar's Latvian instance use a CeleryKubernetes hybrid?
options: Bimodal workload — most tasks are lightweight (Celery efficient) but some need 32GB RAM (K8s isolated)
Latvia has stricter regulations than Lithuania, requiring KubernetesExecutor for regulatory DAGs only
Hybrid is the default for any instance with more than 30 DAGs to balance load between two executors
Hybrid avoids paying licensing fees for Celery alone since Apache charges per-broker for production use
questions.correctAnswer: Bimodal workload — most tasks are lightweight (Celery efficient) but some need 32GB RAM (K8s isolated)
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: pure Celery wastes RAM for the 80% of light tasks. Pure K8s imposes 3-8s startup on lightweight tasks. Hybrid routes by resource profile.

questions.question: What three alternatives should be evaluated before building a custom executor?
options: Custom operators (logic in execute), task wrappers (PythonOperator manages remote), external orchestration
Custom plugins (UI extensions), custom timetables (scheduling), custom XCom backends (data passing)
CeleryExecutor (default), KubernetesExecutor (isolation), LocalExecutor (development simplicity)
Forking Airflow (full control), patching the executor (selective override), upstream PRs (community fix)
questions.correctAnswer: Custom operators (logic in execute), task wrappers (PythonOperator manages remote), external orchestration
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: each alternative is simpler than a custom executor. Build a custom executor only when execution is fundamentally incompatible with built-in models.

questions.question: Why does BalticSolar reclassify a task to a higher resource tier rather than raising its memory limit?
options: High limit-to-request ratios reduce scheduling efficiency — the cluster cannot pack pods reliably
High limit-to-request ratios trigger the regulator's audit requirement for any task above 4GB usage
High limit-to-request ratios are blocked by the Kubernetes API server's admission controller globally
High limit-to-request ratios are charged at 5× the rate by the cloud provider for resource over-commit
questions.correctAnswer: High limit-to-request ratios reduce scheduling efficiency — the cluster cannot pack pods reliably
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: a 1Gi request with 8Gi limit means K8s schedules optimistically but evicts under pressure. Reclassification keeps requests realistic.

questions.question: What is BalticSolar's worker-fleet ownership model?
options: Platform team owns fleet sizing/health/scaling; pipeline teams interact via pools and resource requests
Each pipeline team operates its own worker fleet sized to their DAG count and updated independently
Pipeline teams own day-to-day operations; platform team is consulted only for major capacity changes
Operations team owns workers; platform team owns the scheduler; pipeline teams own only their DAG code
questions.correctAnswer: Platform team owns fleet sizing/health/scaling; pipeline teams interact via pools and resource requests
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: fleet management is infrastructure responsibility. Pipeline teams say 'we need this much capacity'; platform team delivers it.

questions.question: Why does BalticSolar profile executor performance through the dashboard rather than tuning by intuition?
options: Every optimization has a trade-off — only profiling data identifies the highest SLA improvement per cost
Profiling is required by the regulator for any executor change in production environments worldwide
Profiling is the only way to comply with Apache Airflow's recommended performance tuning guidance
Profiling identifies which optimizations have already been applied so duplicates do not stack up over time
questions.correctAnswer: Every optimization has a trade-off — only profiling data identifies the highest SLA improvement per cost
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: increasing max_tis_per_query improves dispatch but loads DB. Reducing zombie interval catches stale tasks but adds overhead. Profile first.

questions.question: What does Lesson 9 say about the cost of maintaining a custom executor?
options: Every hour spent on a custom executor is an hour not improving the platform for the 200 other DAGs
Every hour saved by a custom executor pays for itself within one quarter through reduced infrastructure spend
Every hour spent maintaining a custom executor counts toward the team's Kubernetes certification renewal
Every hour spent on a custom executor is reimbursed by Apache as part of the open-source contribution program
questions.correctAnswer: Every hour spent on a custom executor is an hour not improving the platform for the 200 other DAGs
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: built-ins are maintained by hundreds of community contributors. Custom = your team alone. Use custom operator/wrapper unless truly incompatible.

questions.question: What should pipeline teams need to think about regarding executors when writing DAGs?
options: Nothing — the factory determines executor, resources, and routing from the pipeline config's task type
Everything — pipeline teams must specify queue, executor_config, and pool_override on every task
The country only — pipeline teams pick a queue based on country; the factory handles the rest of routing
The SLA tier only — pipeline teams pick platinum/gold and the platform translates that to an executor
questions.correctAnswer: Nothing — the factory determines executor, resources, and routing from the pipeline config's task type
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: the factory abstracts the executor layer. Pipeline says 'this is a Spark job'; factory translates that into queue, executor_config, resource request.

questions.question: How does Lesson 1 frame integration architecture at scale?
options: As dependency management — cataloging, contracting, isolating failures, governing evolution
As performance optimization — minimizing latency between Airflow tasks and external API calls
As cost optimization — choosing the cheapest provider package that satisfies functional requirements
As security hardening — wrapping every external system call in encryption and authentication layers
questions.correctAnswer: As dependency management — cataloging, contracting, isolating failures, governing evolution
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: 15+ external systems = dependency management. Catalog them, define contracts, isolate failures, govern evolution. Same discipline as schema dependencies.

questions.question: Why does BalticSolar enforce hook-operator separation as an internal API?
options: Hook method changes that break return types break every operator that calls it — same as REST without versioning
Hook-operator separation is mandated by Airflow's plugin system and refused by the loader otherwise
Hook-operator separation halves test runtime because mocked hooks need fewer fixtures than mocked operators
Hook-operator separation prevents the scheduler from caching hook responses across DagRuns inadvertently
questions.correctAnswer: Hook method changes that break return types break every operator that calls it — same as REST without versioning
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: the hook's public methods define the platform's API. Stable signatures, backward-compatible evolution, documented return types — like any internal API.

questions.question: What is the role of an integration contract YAML file at BalticSolar?
options: Defines what the platform expects from the external system and what the system can expect from the platform
Specifies the rate at which the integration is billed by the cloud provider per request invocation
Documents the migration plan from the current external system to its planned replacement vendor
Lists the engineers authorized to modify the integration's connection credentials in HashiCorp Vault
questions.correctAnswer: Defines what the platform expects from the external system and what the system can expect from the platform
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: contract = bidirectional agreement. Same principle as schema contracts in PySpark senior. Detect changes, classify failures, coordinate evolution.

questions.question: BalticSolar's decision framework recommends a custom provider only when which conditions are met?
options: 3+ teams or 5+ DAGs use the integration AND community coverage is below 70% of the team's needs
1+ team uses the integration AND the integration is mentioned in the regulatory audit checklist
10+ teams use the integration AND the platform team has at least 3 engineers available for ownership
Any team uses the integration AND the team is willing to pay the platform team's hourly rate for upkeep
questions.correctAnswer: 3+ teams or 5+ DAGs use the integration AND community coverage is below 70% of the team's needs
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: custom provider costs ~12 days/year. Justified only by enough users (3+ teams or 5+ DAGs) and insufficient community coverage (< 70%).

questions.question: Why does BalticSolar package every custom integration in a single provider rather than separate packages?
options: One release pipeline, one version, one CHANGELOG — and shared infrastructure (base hook, error taxonomy)
Separate packages cannot register connection types in the Airflow UI because of a plugin loader limitation
One package is required by the Airflow community standard for any provider with more than three integrations
Separate packages would expose internal implementation details to teams that should not see other teams' hooks
questions.correctAnswer: One release pipeline, one version, one CHANGELOG — and shared infrastructure (base hook, error taxonomy)
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: trade-off is coupling — Nord Pool change forces ENTSO-E rebuild. Acceptable at monthly release cadence; benefits outweigh.

questions.question: Why is a plugin error worse than a DAG error or a provider error?
options: A plugin error during import crashes the web server, scheduler, and every worker on the instance
A plugin error invalidates the metadata database schema and forces a full restore from backup
A plugin error pages every team's on-call engineer because plugins are owned across teams equally
A plugin error triggers a full Airflow version upgrade because plugins lock the version on import
questions.correctAnswer: A plugin error during import crashes the web server, scheduler, and every worker on the instance
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: plugin errors are instance-wide. DAG errors affect one DAG; provider errors affect DAGs using it; plugin errors affect everything.

questions.question: What does cross-team integration testing verify that provider unit tests do not?
options: The boundary — does the hook's output match the consuming DAG's expectations? Does its perf meet SLA budgets?
The internals — does the hook's auth handshake follow the OAuth 2.0 specification correctly?
The metadata — does the hook register its connection type with the Airflow UI dropdown menu correctly?
The cost — does the hook's call rate stay within the cloud provider's billing tier for that integration?
questions.correctAnswer: The boundary — does the hook's output match the consuming DAG's expectations? Does its perf meet SLA budgets?
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: each team tests their side independently; nobody tests the boundary. Cross-team testing catches mismatches at the contract surface.

questions.question: What is the most valuable output of BalticSolar's quarterly integration review?
options: The deletions — retiring custom integrations replaced by community providers frees engineering time
The additions — approving new custom integrations grows the platform's capability surface area
The metrics — quarterly DAG count and team count for each integration shape the next year's budget
The audits — the regulator requires a list of every external connection point reviewed quarterly
questions.correctAnswer: The deletions — retiring custom integrations replaced by community providers frees engineering time
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: BalticSolar retired 2 integrations year 1, saving ~24 days/year. Without the review, packages grow monotonically.

questions.question: When should you NOT build a custom integration?
options: Integration used by < 3 teams, community provider covers 95%+, or DAG count below the governance threshold
Integration used by > 10 teams because shared usage means too many stakeholders for coordinated changes
Integration on a Lithuanian system because Lithuanian regulations forbid in-house integration code
Integration that uses OAuth because Airflow's community providers handle OAuth poorly across versions
questions.correctAnswer: Integration used by < 3 teams, community provider covers 95%+, or DAG count below the governance threshold
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: governance overhead exceeds savings below the threshold. A PythonOperator wrapper or community provider is the right call.

questions.question: What is the integration architecture's purpose at the platform level?
options: Absorb external changes at the boundary so they do not propagate uncontrolled into the platform
Maximize integration count so the platform supports every external system the alliance might use
Replicate every external system inside the platform so DAGs are insulated from outage at the source
Encrypt every external interaction so the regulator's audit framework reports zero unencrypted bytes
questions.correctAnswer: Absorb external changes at the boundary so they do not propagate uncontrolled into the platform
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: external systems change. Architecture absorbs change at the boundary through contracts, error taxonomies, governance — without it, every external change cascades.

questions.question: What is the goal of reading the Airflow scheduler source code?
options: Trace a production symptom to the code that produces it — targeted reading, not full comprehension
Memorize the entire codebase so any incident can be resolved without consulting documentation later
Identify code paths that the scheduler uses most often so they can be patched for performance gains
Submit upstream patches to the Apache Airflow community as part of the engineer's certification path
questions.correctAnswer: Trace a production symptom to the code that produces it — targeted reading, not full comprehension
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: source reading is a diagnostic skill, not a memorization task. Know that _do_scheduling has 3 phases and trace symptoms to phase logic.

questions.question: What is the cost of a SQL query at the top of a DAG file (e.g., `pd.read_sql(...)` outside a task)?
options: The query runs at parse time on every heartbeat — consumes seconds of every scheduling cycle for nothing
The query runs once at DAG import and is cached forever — but the cached value goes stale across restarts
The query runs at every task instance creation — multiplies parser load by the number of task instances
The query runs only when the DAG is triggered — but the result is invisible to the scheduler dispatcher
questions.correctAnswer: The query runs at parse time on every heartbeat — consumes seconds of every scheduling cycle for nothing
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: parse-time imports run on every heartbeat. With 4 parsing processes and 200+ files, one slow file dominates the parsing budget.

questions.question: How should the DagRun state machine be understood?
options: Bookkeeping — tracks whether a DAG's tasks have been processed for a specific data interval
Workflow engine — orchestrates task execution and resource allocation across all running DAGs
Event sourcing — replays the entire DagRun history during scheduling to derive current state
Reactive system — adjusts task priorities continuously based on real-time external signals
questions.correctAnswer: Bookkeeping — tracks whether a DAG's tasks have been processed for a specific data interval
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: DagRun is bookkeeping. Scheduler creates them, executor runs tasks, state reflects aggregate outcome. Understanding this explains UI behaviors.

questions.question: What is a 'zombie task,' and what causes one?
options: Task in 'running' state without an active heartbeat — worker crashed, OOM, network split, evicted pod
Task in 'failed' state that retries despite exceeding the max retry count — a state machine glitch
Task in 'scheduled' state that the executor refuses to dispatch — a permissions or authorization issue
Task in 'success' state with no XCom value — a serialization failure that lost the return value silently
questions.correctAnswer: Task in 'running' state without an active heartbeat — worker crashed, OOM, network split, evicted pod
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: DB believes task is running but no executor process is. After zombie_detection_interval, scheduler fails it; retries fire if available.

questions.question: Why does the Airflow UI not show which dependency is blocking a 'scheduled' task?
options: DAG TI Slots / Pool Slots / Trigger Rule deps are evaluated at DEBUG log level — not surfaced in the UI
Dependencies cannot be evaluated for running tasks because of metadata DB row locking semantics
Dependencies are encrypted by Airflow's security layer to prevent leaking them across multi-tenant boundaries
Dependencies are computed lazily at the executor level so the scheduler does not know which one blocks
questions.correctAnswer: DAG TI Slots / Pool Slots / Trigger Rule deps are evaluated at DEBUG log level — not surfaced in the UI
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: dep chain output is in DEBUG-level scheduler logs. SaulėGrid runbook: check scheduler DEBUG logs first when tasks are stuck.

questions.question: Why does BalticSolar identify the DAG factory as the most effective scheduler-rent reduction?
options: It consolidates 180 individually-parsed files into 180 YAML configs read by a single factory file
It compiles DAGs ahead of time into Airflow's serialized binary format that skips parsing
It runs DAG parsing on a separate K8s cluster so the scheduler is not blocked by parse-time work
It moves DAG files out of the dags/ folder entirely and into a database table indexed by team
questions.correctAnswer: It consolidates 180 individually-parsed files into 180 YAML configs read by a single factory file
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: DAG dir parsing is the scheduler's rent. Factory consolidates parsing into one optimizable file instead of 180 uncoordinated ones.

questions.question: How does BalticSolar's active-active scheduler HA prevent duplicate task dispatch?
options: PostgreSQL `SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED` — each scheduler locks a batch of tasks no other can see
Redis distributed locks — each scheduler acquires a lock per task before dispatching to the executor
ZooKeeper coordination — only one scheduler is leader and dispatches; the other is a hot standby
Manual partitioning — scheduler 1 handles odd-numbered DAGs and scheduler 2 handles even-numbered
questions.correctAnswer: PostgreSQL `SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED` — each scheduler locks a batch of tasks no other can see
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: row-locking with SKIP LOCKED enables concurrent processing of disjoint task sets. Tested monthly with zero missed/duplicated dispatches over 12 months.

questions.question: What ultimately manifests every scheduling optimization in Modules 1-5?
options: A reduction in the scheduler's per-heartbeat workload — the platform's fundamental resource
A reduction in the metadata database's storage footprint — the platform's storage capacity limit
A reduction in the executor's queue depth — the platform's task dispatch capacity limit
A reduction in the worker fleet's CPU usage — the platform's compute spending baseline cost
questions.correctAnswer: A reduction in the scheduler's per-heartbeat workload — the platform's fundamental resource
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: factory reduces parse time, priority pools reduce eligible task count, lazy imports reduce per-file overhead. All optimizations target heartbeat budget.

questions.question: What does Lesson 9 say a senior engineer recognizes that a mid-level engineer does not?
options: When the scheduler should NOT handle a workload at all — Airflow has design boundaries beyond which a different system fits
When the scheduler can handle 10× more tasks than its documentation suggests — through aggressive tuning of every config
When the scheduler can be patched to fix performance issues — through forking Airflow and applying internal team patches
When the scheduler can be replaced by a custom one — through implementing the BaseExecutor interface differently
questions.correctAnswer: When the scheduler should NOT handle a workload at all — Airflow has design boundaries beyond which a different system fits
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: not every optimization belongs in Airflow. Sub-second dispatch latency, real-time orchestration, millions of tasks/day = different tool.

questions.question: Lesson 10 says the scheduler's power and its limitations come from the same place. Where?
options: The metadata database — state machines, row locking, serialized DAG storage, query latency, table growth
The DAG file directory — single source of truth, deployment simplicity, parse cost, file count overhead
The Python interpreter — flexible task definitions, ecosystem reuse, GIL contention, import cost
The web UI — observability, manual intervention, browser session state, REST API rate limits
questions.correctAnswer: The metadata database — state machines, row locking, serialized DAG storage, query latency, table growth
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: power = simple state model in the DB. Limits = query latency, connection pool, table growth. Module 7 covers the database side.

questions.question: How does Lesson 1 reframe the metadata database?
options: Active working memory — every scheduler query is on the critical path of a scheduling decision
Historical archive — read-only record of past executions consulted during incident investigation
Configuration store — connection passwords, variables, and pool definitions read at boot only
Event stream — append-only log of state transitions consumed by the audit and observability stack
questions.correctAnswer: Active working memory — every scheduler query is on the critical path of a scheduling decision
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: scheduler reads task_instance every heartbeat, dag_run on creation, pool on dispatch. DB latency = scheduling delay regardless of code optimization.

questions.question: Why must the platform team understand PostgreSQL query optimization as an Airflow prerequisite?
options: Two identical Airflow installs can perform very differently based on indexing, statistics, and tuning
Airflow generates raw SQL by hand and the platform team must review every query before deployment
PostgreSQL's query planner blocks Airflow operations that take longer than 10ms in production by default
Airflow refuses to start unless the engineer passes a PostgreSQL certification administered by Apache
questions.correctAnswer: Two identical Airflow installs can perform very differently based on indexing, statistics, and tuning
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: scheduling perf depends on DB perf. EXPLAIN ANALYZE, index tuning, vacuum scheduling are prerequisites for scheduler optimization.

questions.question: When does an XCom value belong in S3 instead of the metadata database?
options: Above ~100KB — payloads larger than this slow scheduler queries through DB I/O bandwidth contention
Above 5MB — Airflow's hard limit beyond which the metadata database refuses to accept the value
Above 1GB — the threshold where PostgreSQL's TOAST mechanism degrades to single-row reads
Above 10KB — anything that does not fit in a single TCP packet should be stored externally
questions.correctAnswer: Above ~100KB — payloads larger than this slow scheduler queries through DB I/O bandwidth contention
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: small payloads (<100KB) stay in the DB; larger payloads bloat the metadata DB and slow scheduling. The custom backend stores ref keys for big values.

questions.question: Why does BalticSolar treat metadata DB maintenance failure as P2?
options: Accumulated performance degradation from a missed maintenance window compounds over subsequent weeks
Lithuanian regulators require quarterly proof that the maintenance DAG ran on schedule for compliance
The maintenance DAG owns the database backup and a missed run leaves the platform without DR coverage
The maintenance DAG triggers the SLA monitor and a missed run blocks every Gold DAG from completing
questions.correctAnswer: Accumulated performance degradation from a missed maintenance window compounds over subsequent weeks
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: 500K dead tuples in task_instance = dependency queries 30-40% slower. Maintenance is a scheduling prerequisite, not housekeeping.

questions.question: Why does BalticSolar rehearse Airflow database migrations on production-volume staging data?
options: 5-second migrations on 1K-row staging take 45 minutes on 1.7M-row production due to index creation
Lithuanian regulations require migration timing to be reported in advance of any version upgrade
Migrations are CPU-bound and the staging cluster has different CPUs than production hardware
Apache Airflow refuses to apply migrations to databases that have not undergone rehearsal first
questions.correctAnswer: 5-second migrations on 1K-row staging take 45 minutes on 1.7M-row production due to index creation
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: production-volume rehearsal predicts duration and surfaces lock contention. Staging refresh from prod backup before every rehearsal.

questions.question: What architectural advantage does Vault provide over DB-stored connections?
options: Credential lifecycle decoupled from Airflow lifecycle — security team rotates without DAG redeployment
Lower query latency for connection lookup — Vault's response time is faster than the metadata database
Stronger encryption — Vault uses AES-256 while Airflow's database stores credentials in plaintext only
Higher availability — Vault's clustering protocol provides 5× the uptime of a managed PostgreSQL service
questions.correctAnswer: Credential lifecycle decoupled from Airflow lifecycle — security team rotates without DAG redeployment
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: DB-stored credentials couple rotation to the platform's deploy lifecycle. Vault decouples them — rotation does not need platform team involvement.

questions.question: How does BalticSolar achieve tamper-evidence for regulatory audit trails?
options: Audit export to compliance warehouse with immutable storage (S3 Object Lock) + per-batch SHA-256 hash
Audit log encryption with a per-event key managed by the security team's hardware security module
Audit log replication across all four Airflow instances so any tampering creates an inconsistency immediately
Audit log signing by the engineer who triggered the action so the regulator can verify identity per event
questions.correctAnswer: Audit export to compliance warehouse with immutable storage (S3 Object Lock) + per-batch SHA-256 hash
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: log table is mutable. Export to immutable S3 + hash comparison detects tampering. Required for VERT/SPRK/Konkurentsiiamet audits.

questions.question: Why is vertical (not horizontal) scaling correct for BalticSolar's metadata database?
options: PostgreSQL on appropriately sized hardware handles millions of rows below 1,000+ DAG / 100K+ task scale
Horizontal scaling is forbidden by Airflow's design and the scheduler refuses to connect to sharded DBs
Vertical scaling is the only option offered by managed PostgreSQL providers in the EU regions used
Horizontal scaling triples the cost per query because of cross-shard coordination overhead at runtime
questions.correctAnswer: PostgreSQL on appropriately sized hardware handles millions of rows below 1,000+ DAG / 100K+ task scale
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: at BalticSolar scale, distributed transactions/query routing/schema sync complexity is not justified. Bigger hardware + IOPS + PgBouncer is the answer.

questions.question: What is the correct mental model for the metadata database's storage capacity?
options: Scarce resource allocated to scheduling — every non-scheduling byte reduces scheduling throughput
Cheap commodity — store any operational data with the platform's other Airflow tables for convenience
Backup target — large XCom values and audit logs help with restoration testing if data is lost
Performance buffer — storing data in the metadata DB makes it faster to query than external storage
questions.correctAnswer: Scarce resource allocated to scheduling — every non-scheduling byte reduces scheduling throughput
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: every byte stored = backup time, vacuum time, query competition. XCom backend, Secrets backend, audit export all keep non-scheduling data out.

questions.question: Lesson 10 says the metadata database's simplicity is both Airflow's strength and its scaling constraint. Why?
options: Single source of truth for state — easy to reason about, but every component shares its capacity
Single Python module per state machine — easy to read, but limits concurrent state transitions
Single SQL dialect — easy to deploy, but limits the database engine choice to PostgreSQL only
Single connection pool — easy to manage, but limits the number of active scheduling threads
questions.correctAnswer: Single source of truth for state — easy to reason about, but every component shares its capacity
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: every component reads/writes the same DB. No distributed state, no eventual consistency. The DB's capacity = the platform's capacity.

questions.question: What is the tenancy model decision actually about?
options: An organizational decision about how teams share infrastructure, propagate failures, and enforce compliance
A software configuration about whether to deploy one Airflow instance or multiple Airflow instances
A licensing decision about whether to pay Apache for multi-tenant features or use the open-source build
A networking decision about whether to use VPC peering or transit gateways between team workloads
questions.correctAnswer: An organizational decision about how teams share infrastructure, propagate failures, and enforce compliance
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: tenancy follows organizational constraints — regulatory data residency, team count, cross-team dependencies. Technology follows the model, not the reverse.

questions.question: Why does BalticSolar's policy say 'no user receives a built-in role directly'?
options: Built-in User can trigger DAGs and view connection strings — too much for an analytics team member
Built-in roles are deprecated and the Airflow community recommends custom roles for every user account
Built-in roles do not work with OAuth authentication so SAML federation breaks for any built-in role user
Built-in roles cost extra licensing fees in the enterprise edition and the open-source edition lacks them
questions.correctAnswer: Built-in User can trigger DAGs and view connection strings — too much for an analytics team member
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: User can trigger DAGs and view connections. Custom roles restrict to team's namespace. Built-ins serve only as base templates.

questions.question: Why does the DAG factory set `access_control` automatically based on the config's owner_team?
options: Aligns DAG visibility/permissions with the namespace convention and ownership registry — automatic correctness
Reduces YAML complexity by eliminating the access_control field from the pipeline config schema entirely
Bypasses the metadata database's RBAC tables for performance reasons by computing permissions in code
Enables the scheduler to skip RBAC evaluation for factory-generated DAGs since the factory pre-validated
questions.correctAnswer: Aligns DAG visibility/permissions with the namespace convention and ownership registry — automatic correctness
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: namespace + RBAC + access_control work together. Factory enforcement = organizational intent technically enforced at every interaction point.

questions.question: What does BalticSolar's three-layer data residency design protect against?
options: Even with one layer breached for maintenance, the other layers continue to prevent unauthorized access
Even when a country regulator audits, the layers reduce the audit's scope to the network layer only
Even when a country instance crashes, the layers ensure data is replicated to the other countries safely
Even when a team adds a new connection, the layers automatically apply the correct country prefix to it
questions.correctAnswer: Even with one layer breached for maintenance, the other layers continue to prevent unauthorized access
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: defense in depth. Infrastructure + connection + RBAC. A relaxed VPN tunnel during debugging does not by itself enable cross-country data access.

questions.question: How do RBAC and Vault policy cover each other's gaps?
options: RBAC blocks UI/API; Vault policy blocks instance access — bypass UI but Vault token still denies cross-country secrets
RBAC blocks read access; Vault policy blocks write access — write protection is independent of read controls
RBAC blocks production; Vault policy blocks staging — environment separation is enforced at both layers
RBAC blocks deletion; Vault policy blocks creation — lifecycle management is split between two systems
questions.correctAnswer: RBAC blocks UI/API; Vault policy blocks instance access — bypass UI but Vault token still denies cross-country secrets
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: a Lithuanian operator bypassing the UI still cannot read Latvian secrets — Vault token policy denies it. A Vault policy mistake is caught by RBAC.

questions.question: What does Lesson 6 say about when compliance work should happen?
options: Continuously — every DAG deploy with access_control, every credential rotation verified, every CI namespace check
Quarterly only — focus on quarterly review instead of distracting engineers with day-to-day compliance overhead
Annually only — once-per-year regulatory submission is sufficient for the platform's compliance requirements
Reactively — only when the regulator requests evidence, with retroactive compilation of audit data
questions.correctAnswer: Continuously — every DAG deploy with access_control, every credential rotation verified, every CI namespace check
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: compliance is continuous. Quarterly review aggregates continuous actions into a report. If only quarterly = 3 months of latent violations.

questions.question: What is BalticSolar's tenant onboarding target?
options: 1 business day from request to first DAG deploy on staging — automation enables 2-3 hours of platform team work
1 business week — gives platform team time to manually configure RBAC, secrets, and pools per team
1 business month — allows for formal training, governance review, and cross-team coordination meetings
1 business quarter — aligns with the quarterly governance review so onboarding gets full team attention
questions.correctAnswer: 1 business day from request to first DAG deploy on staging — automation enables 2-3 hours of platform team work
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: onboarding speed = leading indicator of platform health. If onboarding slows, manual steps have outpaced automation.

questions.question: What does the subscription registry convert?
options: Invisible cross-tenant Dataset dependencies into explicit, governed, and acknowledged relationships
Implicit Dataset URIs into explicit Vault paths so each consumer reads through the secrets backend
Manual cross-tenant data exchanges into REST API calls that the coordinator instance routes by tenant
Cron-scheduled cross-tenant DAGs into Dataset-triggered DAGs to remove timing assumptions globally
questions.correctAnswer: Invisible cross-tenant Dataset dependencies into explicit, governed, and acknowledged relationships
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: without the registry, you discover cross-tenant deps by breaking them. The registry makes deps documented, change impact assessable, breaking changes coordinated.

questions.question: When is enterprise-grade multi-tenancy NOT justified?
options: Fewer than 3 teams, no regulatory residency requirements, and minimal cross-team dependencies
Fewer than 10 DAGs because RBAC overhead is high relative to the platform's surface area
Fewer than 100 users because the audit log volume is too small to justify the warehouse export
Fewer than 5 external integrations because contract validation is overkill at smaller scales
questions.correctAnswer: Fewer than 3 teams, no regulatory residency requirements, and minimal cross-team dependencies
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: enterprise multi-tenancy at 2-team scale = premature optimization. Governance overhead consumes time that should build pipelines.

questions.question: Lesson 10 names three layers of governance. Which set is correct?
options: Technical controls (real-time prevention) + audit (detection) + organizational processes (adaptation)
Network controls + application controls + data controls — each at a different OSI layer
Pre-deployment + production + post-incident — each at a different point in the development lifecycle
Country level + team level + DAG level — each at a different scope of enforcement granularity
questions.correctAnswer: Technical controls (real-time prevention) + audit (detection) + organizational processes (adaptation)
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: prevention + detection + adaptation = immune system. Tech controls alone catch current; all three improve over time.

questions.question: What is the observability challenge at 200+ DAGs?
options: Correlating data that already exists, not collecting more — three signals = one root cause
Storing more metrics than the local Prometheus can hold within its retention window default
Renaming legacy metrics to match the alliance-wide observability naming convention going forward
Replicating dashboards across countries so each country's team has its own copy of every panel
questions.correctAnswer: Correlating data that already exists, not collecting more — three signals = one root cause
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: scheduling delay + DB latency + 15 DAG failures = one cause. Dashboards that show the correlation in 30 seconds vs 30 min of log archaeology.

questions.question: Why is SLA tier assignment governed by the quarterly review rather than self-service?
options: If every team self-assigns platinum, the resource reservation system collapses (priority inflation)
Self-service SLA tiers violate Lithuanian regulations that require quarterly platform team approval
Self-service SLA tiers prevent the scheduler from caching priority weights across DagRuns reliably
Self-service SLA tiers cost an extra licensing fee that the alliance is unwilling to pay annually
questions.correctAnswer: If every team self-assigns platinum, the resource reservation system collapses (priority inflation)
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: tier = resource allocation decision. Higher tier costs other DAGs. Governance prevents priority inflation (Module 3 lesson 9 anti-pattern).

questions.question: What is the purpose of BalticSolar's alert correlation engine?
options: Group related alerts into one incident, route to the right team — prevents 50 alerts from one root cause
Suppress duplicate alerts by keeping only the highest-severity one within a sliding 5-minute window
Translate alerts into PagerDuty-format JSON before delivery to the on-call rotation's notification system
Throttle alert frequency below the regulator's reporting cap of 100 alert events per business day
questions.correctAnswer: Group related alerts into one incident, route to the right team — prevents 50 alerts from one root cause
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: scheduler slow + 15 DAG failures = scheduler is the root cause, not 15 separate incidents. Correlation prevents alert fatigue at platform scale.

questions.question: How do detection patterns progress from reactive to predictive?
options: Task failure (reactive) → duration anomaly (proactive) → scheduling drift (predictive)
Pipeline error (reactive) → SLA breach (proactive) → SLO breach (predictive) — based on customer impact
Manual triage (reactive) → automated runbooks (proactive) → AI-driven mitigation (predictive)
Single-DAG (reactive) → multi-DAG (proactive) → cross-instance (predictive) — based on scope of effect
questions.correctAnswer: Task failure (reactive) → duration anomaly (proactive) → scheduling drift (predictive)
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: further upstream = more time to remediate. Reactive catches what already happened; predictive catches what would happen if no action.

questions.question: Why does BalticSolar's chaos engineering deliberately trigger documented failure modes?
options: Verifies that diagnosis steps work and recovery procedures succeed — playbook assumptions vs production reality
Generates training data for an LLM that auto-resolves common incidents based on past chaos test outcomes
Demonstrates resilience to the regulator during the quarterly compliance review with documented evidence
Reduces the on-call team's monthly compensation since chaos tests count as practice incident response shifts
questions.correctAnswer: Verifies that diagnosis steps work and recovery procedures succeed — playbook assumptions vs production reality
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: 'systemctl restart' is useless if pods don't have systemd. Chaos tests catch playbook-vs-reality gaps. Update the playbook from each test.

questions.question: What does BalticSolar's blameless post-mortem principle redirect investigation toward?
options: From 'who made the mistake' to 'what system allowed the mistake to reach production'
From 'when did this happen' to 'how often has this happened in the last quarter at the platform'
From 'why did this happen' to 'what monetary impact did this have on the alliance's quarterly budget'
From 'how was this fixed' to 'who got paged about this and how quickly did they respond'
questions.correctAnswer: From 'who made the mistake' to 'what system allowed the mistake to reach production'
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: 'engineer deployed bad config' is blame. 'CI didn't validate config against schema' is systemic. Systemic findings produce durable improvements.

questions.question: Why did BalticSolar wait until Month 6 to start chaos engineering?
options: Prerequisites: platform observability, alerting, playbooks, post-mortem process — all four operational first
Lithuanian regulators required formal approval for chaos testing in production-grade systems
The platform team's headcount only reached the threshold needed to run chaos exercises in Month 6
Apache Airflow added native chaos engineering support in version 2.7 which BalticSolar adopted in Month 6
questions.correctAnswer: Prerequisites: platform observability, alerting, playbooks, post-mortem process — all four operational first
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: chaos without observability/alerting/playbooks is just chaos. Damages confidence rather than building it. Wait until the prerequisites work.

questions.question: Why are capacity incidents fully predictable?
options: Resource consumption trends are visible in monitoring, growth rates calculable, threshold dates projectable
Capacity incidents require an external trigger that the platform always logs in advance via StatsD
Capacity incidents only happen during business hours so the on-call team can prepare each morning shift
Capacity incidents are scheduled by the platform team at a fixed cadence to test resilience
questions.correctAnswer: Resource consumption trends are visible in monitoring, growth rates calculable, threshold dates projectable
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: a capacity incident means trends were visible, threshold projectable, and team did not act. Forecasting + quarterly review = organizationally impossible.

questions.question: Why does an alert without a documented response action damage the alerting system?
options: Trains the engineer to ignore alerts — alert fatigue spiral is hard to reverse once it starts
Violates the regulator's audit requirement that every alert have a documented owner per incident
Slows the scheduler because the alerter holds a database row lock during alert generation overhead
Increases the cost of PagerDuty because the per-alert pricing tier scales with the response time
questions.correctAnswer: Trains the engineer to ignore alerts — alert fatigue spiral is hard to reverse once it starts
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: every ignored alert makes the next 5% more likely to be ignored. Engineer learns 'probably another false positive'. Spiral is hard to reverse.

questions.question: Lesson 10 calls observability what part of the platform?
options: The nervous system — sensory network that detects pain, reports it, enables reflexive response
The heart — pumps metrics through the platform so each component receives operational data
The skeleton — provides structure that holds the platform's components in their proper places
The skin — the outer surface that protects the platform from environmental factors externally
questions.correctAnswer: The nervous system — sensory network that detects pain, reports it, enables reflexive response
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: architecture = skeleton. Factory + scheduling = muscle. Database + scheduler = brain. Governance = immune system. Observability = nervous system.

questions.question: What does Lesson 1 say about platform architecture phases?
options: Architectures fit current scale; phases are natural consequences of growth — not failures of the previous design
Architectures are stable for 5+ years; phase transitions only occur due to major regulatory changes
Architectures should be designed for the imagined final state to avoid costly rewrites later
Architectures must change every quarter to stay current with the latest Airflow community recommendations
questions.correctAnswer: Architectures fit current scale; phases are natural consequences of growth — not failures of the previous design
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: don't over-engineer for 1000 DAGs at 200 scale. Senior engineers design for current scale + awareness of next inflection.

questions.question: Why is BalticSolar's 48-hour canary window for Airflow upgrades not optional?
options: Two issues in 4 upgrades that would have hit all 3 instances simultaneously were caught on Lithuanian canary
Lithuanian regulations mandate a 48-hour proving period before any version change becomes effective
Apache Airflow's release notes require canary deployment and the community refuses support otherwise
The metadata database's replication lag is exactly 48 hours so the DB is never consistent before then
questions.correctAnswer: Two issues in 4 upgrades that would have hit all 3 instances simultaneously were caught on Lithuanian canary
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: canary on LT, evaluate, then LV/EE. Caught Dataset trigger dedup change (15 DAGs) and PgBouncer exhaustion. Both prevented alliance-wide breaches.

questions.question: What partnership produces realistic capacity plans?
options: Engineering provides forecasts and cost models; finance provides budget envelope and prioritization criteria
Engineering provides the entire plan; finance signs off on whatever number engineering puts forward
Engineering follows finance's budget cap; the team scales infrastructure to whatever budget finance grants
Engineering and finance produce independent plans; whoever's number is lower wins for the next fiscal year
questions.correctAnswer: Engineering provides forecasts and cost models; finance provides budget envelope and prioritization criteria
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: engineering alone = no business justification. Finance alone = no engineering reality. Quarterly review with both teams + shared budget model.

questions.question: How does DAG deprecation parallel API deprecation?
options: Announce → migration path → monitor adoption → remove only after all consumers have migrated
Pause immediately → wait one week → delete forcibly so consumers learn to keep their tooling current
Mark as deprecated in code → ignore until the next major version → bulk-remove during the upgrade
Reroute traffic to a new DAG name → keep the old one running indefinitely as a permanent fallback
questions.correctAnswer: Announce → migration path → monitor adoption → remove only after all consumers have migrated
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: 90-day window + consumer tracking. Removing before all consumers migrated = data platform's equivalent of breaking a production API.

questions.question: What does Lesson 5 warn about missing ADRs?
options: An ADR from Month 3 cannot be accurately written in Month 12 — context, alternatives, rationale fade
An ADR from Month 3 cannot be approved by the regulator without the engineer's contemporaneous signature
An ADR from Month 3 must be re-written every quarter to stay aligned with current platform practices
An ADR from Month 3 is automatically deleted by the document store's retention policy after 9 months
questions.correctAnswer: An ADR from Month 3 cannot be accurately written in Month 12 — context, alternatives, rationale fade
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: documentation debt past a point cannot be recovered. Quarterly completeness scoring with dedicated catch-up sprints prevents this.

questions.question: What is the measure of a mature platform team?
options: How many DAGs other teams can build without them — independence enabled, not dependence created
How many DAGs the platform team itself builds per quarter for downstream consumer teams
How many incidents the platform team resolves single-handedly without escalating to other teams
How many Apache Airflow contributions the platform team makes to the upstream community per year
questions.correctAnswer: How many DAGs other teams can build without them — independence enabled, not dependence created
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: a team that builds for 10 teams creates a dependency. A team that builds the factory + CI + monitoring + onboarding creates independence.

questions.question: What did BalticSolar's first DR test reveal about backups?
options: Backup worked at staging size; restoration script timed out at production volume — backup ≠ restoration
Backup worked but the encryption key was lost — restoration succeeded but data was unreadable afterward
Backup worked but the Airflow version had advanced — restoration succeeded but DAGs failed to parse
Backup worked but the disk filled up — restoration was incomplete because the staging disk was too small
questions.correctAnswer: Backup worked at staging size; restoration script timed out at production volume — backup ≠ restoration
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: untested DR is hope. Production-volume rehearsal proves the procedure works at scale, not just that backups exist.

questions.question: What does BalticSolar's tool-agnostic governance framework enable?
options: Migration changes the implementation, not the organization — RBAC, SLA tiers, contracts transfer to a new tool
Migration changes the organization, not the implementation — the platform's components stay in place forever
Migration becomes impossible because the platform is now too tightly coupled to Airflow's specific features
Migration becomes automated by the cloud provider's tooling without human involvement at the platform layer
questions.correctAnswer: Migration changes the implementation, not the organization — RBAC, SLA tiers, contracts transfer to a new tool
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: governance describes WHAT the platform does, not HOW Airflow implements it. Hypothetical migration changes implementation while governance survives.

questions.question: What does Lesson 9 say is the most durable contribution of a senior engineer?
options: Organizational artifacts (ADRs, onboarding docs, governance, team structure) outlast personnel changes
Technical artifacts (factory code, scheduling architecture, observability dashboards) define the platform
Cost reductions (dependency retirement, efficiency optimizations, vendor renegotiations) prove value
Open-source contributions (PRs to Apache Airflow, custom executors, plugins) credit the engineer's career
questions.correctAnswer: Organizational artifacts (ADRs, onboarding docs, governance, team structure) outlast personnel changes
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: tech artifacts collapse without org context. Platform that evolves after architect leaves = well-designed organization, not just well-coded software.

questions.question: Lesson 10 frames the journey from platform engineer to platform architect. What does the architect now know how to do?
options: Design the platform that makes DAG dev/deploy/ops reliable for 10 teams across 3 countries
Write better DAGs than every other engineer in the alliance using advanced TaskFlow patterns and decorators
Operate the existing Airflow installation without ever needing to escalate incidents to the platform team
Configure Kubernetes clusters and PostgreSQL replicas without involving the infrastructure team for changes
questions.correctAnswer: Design the platform that makes DAG dev/deploy/ops reliable for 10 teams across 3 countries
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: arch from M1, factory M2, scheduling M3, executor M4, integration M5, scheduler M6, DB M7, governance M8, observability M9, lifecycle M10 = complete toolkit.
